title: R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course
text: Welcome

title: R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course
text:
Why this course exists
Most R textbooks teach syntax first and hope the motivation follows. This course does the opposite. Every page opens with a problem you might actually be handed as a public health intern, a research assistant, an epidemiology student, or a data analyst on a health department team — and you learn exactly the R you need, at the moment you need it, to solve it.
You will not find a chapter that starts with “Today we will learn the filter() function.” Instead you will find a supervisor asking you to find every participant over 65 with hypertension, and filter() will show up as the tool that gets you out of that jam.

title: R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course
text:
Who this is for
This course assumes zero prior programming experience. If you have never written a line of code in your life but you can think through a problem logically, you are the target reader. It is built for students and professionals in:
Public health, epidemiology, biostatistics, clinical research, health data analysis, survey research, and population health.
By the final capstone level, the material assumes you have worked through everything before it — the course is cumulative, not a reference manual to jump around in on your first pass.

title: R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course
text:
How each lesson works
Every lesson in this book follows the same rhythm, because the rhythm itself is what builds problem-solving skill:

1. Real-World Problem — a short, realistic scenario from health research.


2. Your Mission — the concrete task you need to accomplish.


3. Think Before Coding — a few questions to reason through before touching the keyboard.


4. The R Concept — the one new idea you need, explained plainly: what it does, its syntax, its arguments, and what usually goes wrong.

5. Demonstration — runnable R code showing the concept in isolation.
6. Explore — small modifications you make to that code to test your understanding.
7. Solve the Problem — you return to the opening scenario and solve it completely.
8. Interpret the Result — the output translated into a plain-language sentence a supervisor or reader would understand.

9. Common Mistakes — errors beginners reliably make with this concept, and why they happen.

10. Practice Problem — a new, similar problem for you to solve with support.
11. Challenge Problem — a harder variant you solve with less scaffolding.
12. Key Takeaways — two to four sentences worth remembering.

13. Knowledge Check — multiple-choice, true/false, predict-the-output, find-the-error, and write-the-code questions, with answers hidden behind a toggle so you can self-test honestly.

title: R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course
text:
The dataset you’ll live in
Rather than switching example datasets every page, most of this course reuses a single running dataset: a simulated community health survey of 100 participants, with age, sex, county, systolic and diastolic blood pressure, BMI, and smoking status — including realistic missing values. You’ll meet it for the first time in Level 1 and return to it constantly, so that learning R and learning to think about a real dataset happen together. The file lives at data/participants.csv.
By Level 15 you’ll be running full analyses — and eventually independent capstone projects — on data structured just like it.

title: R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do
By the end, starting from zero, you will be able to navigate R and RStudio, write and debug your own R code, understand R’s core data structures, import and clean real-world health data, manipulate data fluently with the tidyverse, build publication-quality figures, write your own functions and automate repetitive analysis, run and interpret the statistical methods most common in health research, work with longitudinal, survey, and time-to-event data, and produce a fully reproducible research report from raw data to final results — in Quarto, from scratch.
Let’s start with the question every beginner has: what is R, actually, and why are we bothering?

title: Level 1 — Getting Started with R
text:
What you’ll build toward
Every page in this level adds one small piece, and by the last page you’ll combine all of them to solve the same running problem: your supervisor needs the average age of the participants in a community health survey, and you need to get that number out of R.
Along the way you’ll learn what R and RStudio actually are, how to work in the console and in scripts, how R represents numbers, text, true/false values, and missing data, how to use built-in functions, how to get help when you’re stuck, and how to import a real data file.

title: Level 1 — Getting Started with R
text:
Topics in this level

What is R? What is RStudio?
The RStudio interface
Installing and loading packages
The Console vs. an R script
Comments
Basic arithmetic
The assignment operator and objects
Naming objects well
Data types: numeric, character, logical
Missing values (NA)
Basic functions
Getting help in R
Working directories
Importing your first real dataset

By the end of this level, you’ll import data/participants.csv — a simulated community health survey — and report the mean age of its 100 participants, correctly handling the missing values hiding in it. Let’s begin.

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.1 The R Concept
R is a free, open-source programming language built specifically for statistics, data analysis, and graphics. It was created by statisticians, for statisticians — which is why it’s the default choice in public health, epidemiology, biostatistics, and academic research more broadly.
A few things make R different from a spreadsheet:
R is not the app with the blue icon you click to open. That’s a separate program called RStudio, which you’ll meet on the next page. R is the language and engine underneath — RStudio is the workspace you’ll actually sit in.

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
You write instructions, not clicks. Instead of selecting a range of cells and clicking “AVERAGE,” you write a line of code that says, in effect, “take this data and compute its mean.” That line can be saved, reused, and rerun on next month’s data in seconds.
It’s reproducible. Anyone with your code and your data can get the exact same result. A colleague reviewing your Excel file has to guess which cells you clicked; a colleague reading your R script can see exactly what you did.
It’s built for data, not documents. R has no concept of a “sheet” with 500 unrelated things on it. It’s designed around clean, structured data and the operations researchers actually need: summarizing, filtering, modeling, plotting.
It’s free and extensible. R itself is free, and thousands of free add-ons (called packages) extend it — for reading messy Excel files, running survival analysis, mapping disease outbreaks, and far more.

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.2 Demonstration
Every R session, however small, starts with running code. Let’s run the simplest possible line: basic arithmetic.
# R can act as a calculator right out of the box
2 + 2
[1] 4
That [1] 4 is R’s answer. The [1] just means “this is result number 1” — you’ll see why that notation matters once you work with longer lists of numbers.
Here’s a second line, doing something closer to what your supervisor actually wants: averaging a few numbers.
# The average of four ages, computed directly
(34 + 51 + 62 + 29) / 4
[1] 44

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.3 Explore
Try changing the numbers in the calculation above to four different ages. Then try adding a fifth age to the sum — remember to also change the number you divide by. What happens if you forget to update the divisor?

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.4 Solve the Problem
You don’t yet have the tools to import your supervisor’s actual 100-participant file — that’s coming in a later page. But you can already answer the conceptual question your supervisor implicitly asked: is R the right tool for this, and what will doing this work in R actually look like?
Yes — R is exactly the right tool. Once your data is loaded into R (Level 1, later pages), computing a mean will take a single line of code, mean(), no matter how many participants there are. Whether it’s 100 or 100,000 rows, the code doesn’t get any harder.

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.5 Interpret the Result
When you ran 2 + 2 and got [1] 4, you weren’t just testing a calculator — you confirmed that R is installed, running, and ready to take instructions. That’s the same confirmation a much bigger analysis needs: R evaluates your code and hands back an answer, every time, the same way.

Common Mistakes

Confusing R and RStudio. People often say “open R” when they mean “open RStudio.” You’ll always work inside RStudio, which runs R for you behind the scenes.
Assuming R is only for expert statisticians. R is used by beginners and experts alike — the language scales with you.
Expecting a menu-driven experience. If you’re used to Excel’s toolbar, the biggest adjustment is trusting that a line of text is the instruction, not a shortcut to one.

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.6 Practice Problem
List three tasks in your own field (or one you’re interested in — clinical research, survey analysis, health policy) where you currently rely on Excel or manual calculation, and that could instead be done with reusable, written instructions.

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.7 Challenge Problem
Write two or three sentences, in your own words, explaining to a classmate who has never coded why a health department might choose R over Excel for analyzing a 10,000-row patient dataset. Be specific about at least one concrete advantage.

title: 1  What Is R?
text:
1.8 Key Takeaways

R is a free, open-source programming language built for statistics and data analysis.
Working in R means writing instructions rather than clicking through menus — which makes your work reusable and reproducible.
R and RStudio are not the same thing; R is the language, RStudio is the workspace (next page).


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. R is best described as:

A spreadsheet program
A programming language for statistics and data analysis
A database management system
A word processor

True or false. R and RStudio are two names for the exact same program.
Predict the output. What will the following code print?
10 * 3 - 5
Write the code. Write one line of R code that calculates the average of the numbers 12, 18, and 24 without using any function — just arithmetic.



Show answers


b) A programming language for statistics and data analysis.
False. R is the underlying language; RStudio is a separate application (an IDE) that makes R easier to use.
[1] 25 (10 × 3 = 30, then 30 − 5 = 25).
(12 + 18 + 24) / 3, which evaluates to [1] 18.

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.1 The R Concept
RStudio is an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for R. That’s a general term for an application that wraps a programming language in helpful tools: a place to write and organize code, a way to see your data and objects, a place plots appear, and a way to browse files — all without leaving the app.
Concretely, RStudio gives you:
You’ll tour each of these panes in detail on the next page. For now, the key idea is: R does the computing; RStudio gives you a workspace to do that computing in. Installing R alone gives you the engine with no dashboard. Installing RStudio (in addition to R, not instead of it) gives you the dashboard, the windows, and the seatbelt.

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
A Source pane, where you write and save scripts (reusable files of R code).
A Console pane, where code actually runs and results appear.
An Environment pane, listing every object you’ve created (like a running “what’s currently loaded” panel).
A combined Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer pane, for browsing your project folder, viewing charts, managing installed add-ons, and reading documentation.








A note on Posit



RStudio is made by a company called Posit. You may see “RStudio” and “Posit” used somewhat interchangeably in newer documentation — they refer to the same IDE.

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.2 Demonstration
Once RStudio is open, you can confirm both R and RStudio are working by asking R to report its own version.
# Ask R which version of itself is running
R.version.string
[1] "R version 4.4.1 (2024-06-14)"
You can also check which packages come built into R by default without installing anything extra.
# A quick built-in function - more on functions later in this level
getRversion()
[1] '4.4.1'

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.3 Explore
If RStudio is installed on your machine, open it and locate the version number yourself: click Help > About RStudio in the menu bar. Compare that to the R version reported by the code above — notice they’re reported separately, because they’re genuinely two different pieces of software with two different version numbers.

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.4 Solve the Problem
Your classmates were right: you need both pieces of software, installed separately, working together.

R is installed first — it’s the calculation engine, downloaded from CRAN (the Comprehensive R Archive Network).
RStudio is installed second — it’s the application you actually open day-to-day, and it automatically finds and uses the R installation on your machine.

From this point in the course forward, “opening R” always means opening RStudio.

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.5 Interpret the Result
Seeing R.version.string print a real version number confirms that RStudio has successfully connected to an R installation on your computer. If that line had produced an error instead, it would mean R itself wasn’t installed correctly — a problem to fix before writing any further code.

Common Mistakes

Installing only RStudio. RStudio is not R — without R installed underneath it, RStudio has no engine to run and will show an error on startup.
Installing them in the wrong order. Install R first, then RStudio; RStudio looks for an existing R installation when it starts.
Expecting to “code in RStudio.” You write code in RStudio, but RStudio hands that code to R to actually execute — RStudio itself doesn’t understand R code on its own.

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.6 Practice Problem
Without running any code, describe in one sentence the difference between what R does and what RStudio does — as if explaining it to a classmate who just asked “wait, aren’t those the same thing?”

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.7 Challenge Problem
Look up (or recall) one other IDE used for a different programming language (for example, PyCharm for Python, or VS Code for many languages). Name one feature it likely shares with RStudio, based on what you now know an IDE provides.

title: 2  What Is RStudio?
text:
2.8 Key Takeaways

RStudio is an IDE — a workspace built around R, not a replacement for R.
You must install R first, then RStudio, in that order.
From here on, “using R” in this course means working inside RStudio.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. RStudio is:

A different programming language than R
An IDE that runs on top of R
A cloud storage service
A type of R package

True or false. You can run RStudio without ever installing R.
Find the error. A student says: “I installed RStudio, so I don’t need R.” What’s wrong with this statement?
Write the code. Write the line of R code from this page that asks R to report its own version as a string.



Show answers


b) An IDE that runs on top of R.
False. RStudio requires a working R installation underneath it; it has no engine of its own.
RStudio needs R installed separately — it’s the interface, not the engine. Without R, RStudio has nothing to run your code with.
R.version.string

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.1 The R Concept
RStudio’s default layout has four panes, usually arranged as four quadrants:
The distinction that trips up beginners most: the Source pane is for permanent work, and the Console is for temporary work. Anything you want to keep — and reuse tomorrow — belongs in a script in the Source pane, not typed directly into the Console.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
Source pane (top-left) — where you write and edit R scripts (.R files) and Quarto documents (.qmd files, like the one you’re reading now was written in). Code here is saved and reusable. If this pane isn’t visible, it’s because no file is currently open.
Console pane (bottom-left, or left side if no file is open) — where code actually executes. You can type a line directly here and press Enter to run it immediately, but nothing here is saved automatically.
Environment / History pane (top-right) — the Environment tab lists every object currently loaded in your session (data you’ve imported, values you’ve calculated). The History tab logs every command you’ve run.
Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer pane (bottom-right) — a multi-purpose pane: Files browses your project folder, Plots displays any chart you create, Packages lists installed add-ons, Help shows documentation, and Viewer displays rendered HTML output.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.2 Demonstration
You don’t need special code to “see” panes — but you can generate something in each of the three panes that respond to code, to connect the concept to something concrete.
# Running this creates an object -> it will appear in the Environment pane
intern_name <- "Jordan"

# Running this prints text -> the output appears in the Console pane
print(intern_name)
[1] "Jordan"
If you ran that code inside a script in the Source pane (rather than typing directly in the Console), you would see: the code itself remains visible and saved in the Source pane, the printed result [1] "Jordan" appears in the Console, and intern_name appears listed in the Environment pane with its current value.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.3 Explore
If you have RStudio open, run the two lines above and locate intern_name in your Environment pane. Then click the History tab (next to Environment) and confirm the same line of code is logged there.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.4 Solve the Problem
Now “check the Environment pane” and “look in the Console” have concrete meanings:

“Check the Environment pane” means: look at the top-right section, which lists intern_name (and every other object you’ve created).
“Look in the Console” means: look at the pane where your code’s printed output — like [1] "Jordan" — actually appears.

You now know exactly where to look for each instruction your supervisor gives you.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.5 Interpret the Result
Seeing intern_name listed in the Environment pane after running the code confirms the object was successfully created and is currently available for you to use in later code — the Environment pane is effectively a live inventory of everything you’ve built so far in your session.

Common Mistakes

Doing all work in the Console and never saving a script. Anything typed only in the Console vanishes when you close RStudio (or is easy to lose track of in the History). Important work belongs in a saved script.
Confusing the Environment pane with your saved files. The Environment pane shows what’s currently loaded in memory — closing RStudio without saving clears it, even though your script file itself is unaffected.
Not knowing where plots go. New users often expect a plot to appear in the Console; it will instead appear in the Plots tab of the bottom-right pane.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.6 Practice Problem
Without running any code, match each task to the pane that handles it: (1) writing a reusable analysis script, (2) viewing a bar chart you just created, (3) seeing a list of the data objects you’ve loaded so far, (4) reading the documentation for a function.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.7 Challenge Problem
Explain, in your own words, why “type your entire analysis directly into the Console and never save a script” is a risky habit for someone doing public health research where results may need to be reproduced or audited later.

title: 3  The RStudio Interface
text:
3.8 Key Takeaways

RStudio has four main panes: Source (write/save code), Console (run code), Environment/History (see objects and command history), and Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer (browse, view, and reference).
Permanent work belongs in the Source pane as a saved script; the Console is for quick, temporary checks.
The Environment pane is your live inventory of everything currently loaded in your session.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. Where would you look to see a chart you just created?

Source pane
Console pane
Plots tab
History tab

True or false. Code typed only into the Console is automatically saved when you close RStudio.
Find the error. A student writes their entire semester’s analysis directly in the Console, never opening a script file. What problem will they eventually run into?
Write the code. Write one line of R code that would create an object called city holding the text "Riverdale", which would then appear in the Environment pane.



Show answers


c) The Plots tab, in the bottom-right pane.
False. Console work is not saved automatically; only scripts saved in the Source pane persist as files.
They will lose their work (or lose track of exactly what they ran) as soon as RStudio closes or the History becomes too long to search — with no reusable, reviewable record of their analysis.
city <- "Riverdale"

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.1 The R Concept
Base R — what you get immediately after installing R — includes a solid set of built-in functions, but it doesn’t include everything. Packages are add-on collections of functions, data, and documentation that extend what R can do. Anyone can write and publish one; most official packages are hosted on CRAN (the Comprehensive R Archive Network).
Working with a package is a two-step process, and beginners often only do one of the two steps:
Notice the quoting difference: install.packages() requires the package name in quotes ("readxl"), because it’s just a piece of text identifying what to download. library() conventionally doesn’t require quotes, though quoting it also works.

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
Install it — once per computer.
install.packages("readxl")
This downloads the package and stores it on your machine permanently. You do not need to repeat this every time you use R — only when installing for the first time, or updating later.
Load it — once per R session.
library(readxl)
This tells your current R session “make this package’s functions available right now.” Every time you close and reopen RStudio, you need to load the package again with library() — but you do not need to reinstall it.

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.2 Demonstration
# Step 1: install the package (only needs to be run once, ever, per computer)
install.packages("readxl")
# Step 2: load the package (needs to be run once per R session)
library(readxl)
Once loaded, functions from that package — like read_excel() — become available exactly as if they were built into R.
# Now this function exists and is ready to use
survey_data <- read_excel("data/survey.xlsx")
You can check which packages are already installed on your machine without loading anything:
# Lists every package installed locally (long output - often dozens or hundreds of rows)
installed.packages()[, "Package"] |> head()

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.3 Explore
What do you think happens if you run library(readxl) without ever running install.packages("readxl") first, on a computer where it’s never been installed? Try to predict the error message before you test it — it will say something to the effect of “there is no package called ‘readxl’.”

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.4 Solve the Problem
The could not find function "read_excel" error happened because the tutorial’s code assumed the readxl package was already loaded — but loading it requires two things to have happened first: the package must be installed on your computer, and library() must have been run in your current session. Since you’d done neither, R had no idea what read_excel meant.
The fix:
install.packages("readxl")  # only needed once
library(readxl)              # needed every session
survey_data <- read_excel("data/survey.xlsx")

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.5 Interpret the Result
Once library(readxl) runs without error, it means the package’s functions — including read_excel() — are now available in your current session. If install.packages() had failed (for example, due to no internet connection), library() would fail too, since there’d be nothing installed to load.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting to load the package each new session. Installing is permanent; loading is not. A fresh RStudio session needs library() again even if you installed the package last week.
Reinstalling every time. Running install.packages() at the top of every script is unnecessary and slow — install once, then just library() in future sessions.
Quoting inconsistency. Forgetting the quotes in install.packages(readxl) (missing quotes) causes an error, since R tries to interpret readxl as an object rather than a package name.
Typos in package names. install.packages("read_xl") will fail silently with a “package not available” message if the actual package is spelled readxl (no underscore).

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.6 Practice Problem
Write the two lines of code needed to install and then load a package called janitor (a real, commonly used package for cleaning messy data), assuming it has never been installed on this computer before.

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.7 Challenge Problem
A classmate says their script works perfectly on their own laptop but produces could not find function errors on yours, even though you’re both running the identical script. List two possible explanations related to what you learned on this page, and how you’d check each one.

title: 4  Installing and Loading Packages
text:
4.8 Key Takeaways

Packages extend R with additional functions, data, and tools beyond what comes built in.
install.packages("name") downloads a package once per computer; library(name) loads it once per session.
Forgetting library() — not forgetting to reinstall — is the far more common cause of “could not find function” errors in day-to-day work.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. Which line only needs to be run once, ever, on a given computer?

library(readxl)
install.packages("readxl")
Both need to run every session
Neither needs quotes

True or false. If you restart RStudio, previously installed packages must be reinstalled before you can use them again.
Find the error.
install.packages(readxl)
Write the code. Write the two lines needed to install and load a package called lubridate.



Show answers


b) install.packages("readxl") — installation is a one-time, per-computer action.
False. Installed packages remain installed; you only need to run library() again to load them into the new session.
The package name is missing quotes. It should be install.packages("readxl").
install.packages("lubridate") then library(lubridate).

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.1 The R Concept
The Console is the pane where R code runs immediately and directly — you type a line, press Enter, and see the result right away. Nothing you type there is automatically saved anywhere as a file. This makes the Console perfect for:
It’s a poor choice for anything you’ll want to rerun, hand to a colleague, or revisit next month — for that, you want a script (next page).
You can tell you’re in the Console because it has a > prompt at the start of the line, waiting for input. Type your code after the > and press Enter to run it.

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
Quick, one-off calculations
Checking what a function does before using it in a real script
Peeking at an object’s value in the middle of a longer analysis

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.2 Demonstration
Typed directly into the Console:
> 102 / 120 * 100
[1] 85
You can also press the up arrow key in the Console to recall the previous command, edit it, and rerun it — useful when your supervisor immediately follows up with “okay, now what if it’s 110 out of 120?”
> 110 / 120 * 100
[1] 91.66667

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.3 Explore
In the Console, calculate what percentage 18 out of 120 represents — the dropout rate rather than the completion rate. Then use the up-arrow to recall that line and change 18 to 25. Notice how much faster this is than retyping the whole line.

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.4 Solve the Problem
Back in the meeting, you type directly into the Console:
> 102 / 120 * 100
[1] 85
You answer immediately: “85% of participants completed the study.” No file needed, no saving required — exactly the right use of the Console.

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.5 Interpret the Result
85 means that of the 120 enrolled participants, 85 out of every 100 (equivalently, 102 out of 120) completed the study — an 85% completion rate, which is generally considered a strong retention rate in longitudinal health research.

Common Mistakes

Doing important, reusable analysis only in the Console. If you’ll need this calculation again — for a report, for next month’s update — put it in a script instead (next page).
Losing track of what was run. The Console’s History tab keeps a record, but it’s not as organized or intentional as a saved script with comments explaining each step.
Forgetting the calculation entirely once the Console is cleared or RStudio is closed. Nothing in the Console is saved unless you explicitly copy it elsewhere.

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.6 Practice Problem
In the Console, calculate the percentage of a study where 340 out of 400 participants returned for a 6-month follow-up visit.

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.7 Challenge Problem
Your supervisor now asks a follow-up: “What if we’d also included 40 participants who transferred to another clinic?” Using the up-arrow to build on your previous answer, calculate the new completion percentage out of a total of 440 enrolled, assuming the same 340 completed.

title: 5  Using the Console
text:
5.8 Key Takeaways

The Console runs code immediately but does not save it as a file.
Use the Console for quick, one-off questions — not for analysis you’ll need to reproduce or share.
The up-arrow key recalls previous commands in the Console, which speeds up rapid back-and-forth questions.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. The Console is best suited for:

Long-term, reusable analysis scripts
Quick, one-off calculations and checks
Storing your final report
Installing RStudio itself

True or false. Code typed into the Console is automatically saved as a file when you close RStudio.
Predict the output.
45 / 60 * 100
Write the code. Write the Console command to calculate what percentage 7 is of 28.



Show answers


b) Quick, one-off calculations and checks.
False. Console input is not saved as a file; only scripts you explicitly save are preserved.
[1] 75
7 / 28 * 100, which evaluates to [1] 25.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.1 The R Concept
An R script is a plain text file, saved with a .R extension, that holds a sequence of R commands. Unlike the Console, a script is a permanent, editable, shareable file — closer to a Word document than to a chat window.
To create one in RStudio:
File > New File > R Script (or the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+N on Windows, Cmd+Shift+N on Mac)
This opens a blank file in the Source pane. You write your code there, line by line, and then run it using:
Crucially, writing a line in a script does not run it automatically — you still need to explicitly run it, exactly like the Console, but now the instructions themselves live in a saved file you control.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
Ctrl+Enter (Cmd+Return on Mac) — runs just the current line (or a highlighted selection), sending it to the Console.
The Source button — runs the entire script from top to bottom at once.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.2 Demonstration
Here’s a short script, as it would appear in the Source pane:
# completion_rate.R
# Calculates study completion rate for the community health survey

enrolled <- 120
completed <- 102

completion_rate <- completed / enrolled * 100
completion_rate
Running this line by line (Ctrl+Enter on each line) sends each command to the Console in turn, and you’d see:
[1] 85
Saved as completion_rate.R, this file can be reopened any time, and every line rerun exactly as written.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.3 Explore
Open a new R script and type the same four lines above. Change the values of enrolled and completed to this month’s actual numbers, then run the whole script using the Source button rather than line-by-line. Confirm you get an updated result without retyping any of the structure.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.4 Solve the Problem
Because last month’s calculation lived only in the Console, it’s gone. Going forward, you save every calculation as a script:
# completion_rate.R
enrolled <- 130
completed <- 109

completion_rate <- completed / enrolled * 100
completion_rate
[1] 83.84615
Now, next month, you’ll simply reopen completion_rate.R, update the two numbers, and rerun — no need to remember or retype anything.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.5 Interpret the Result
An 83.8% completion rate for this month, computed from a saved, reusable file rather than a one-time Console calculation, means this exact process can be repeated identically every month going forward — which is the entire point of moving from Console to script.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting to save the script. A script only protects your work once it’s actually saved (Ctrl+S / Cmd+S) — an unsaved script is just as fragile as unsaved Console work.
Confusing “writing” a line with “running” it. Typing a line of code in a script does nothing until you run it (Ctrl+Enter or Source) — the code just sits there as text until executed.
Running lines out of order. If a script depends on earlier lines (like enrolled and completed existing before completion_rate is calculated), running lines out of sequence will cause errors or wrong results.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.6 Practice Problem
Create a script called dropout_rate.R that calculates the dropout rate (as a percentage) for a study where 45 out of 300 enrolled participants dropped out. Include the object names enrolled, dropped, and dropout_rate.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.7 Challenge Problem
Extend your dropout_rate.R script to also calculate the completion rate in the same script, using the same enrolled and dropped objects (hint: completed participants = enrolled − dropped). Make sure both results print when you run the whole script with Source.

title: 6  Creating an R Script
text:
6.8 Key Takeaways

An R script (.R file) is a saved, reusable sequence of commands — unlike Console input, it persists after RStudio closes.
Writing a line of code and running it are two separate steps; use Ctrl+Enter for one line or Source for the whole file.
Save early and often — an unsaved script offers no real advantage over the Console.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What file extension does a standard R script use?

.rscript
.R
.rmd
.txt

True or false. Typing a line of code into a script automatically runs it.
Find the error. A student writes a full script, closes RStudio without saving, and reopens it the next day expecting their code to still be there.
Write the code. Write the keyboard action (not R code) used to run just the current line in a script.



Show answers


b) .R
False. Writing code in a script only stores it as text; you must explicitly run it with Ctrl+Enter (a line) or Source (the whole script).
The script was never saved, so none of it persists — RStudio does not auto-save scripts by default; the student must save the file (Ctrl+S) before closing.
Ctrl+Enter (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+Return (Mac).

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.1 The R Concept
A comment in R is any text following a # symbol on a line. R completely ignores everything after # — comments exist purely for humans reading the code, not for R itself.
Comments are useful for:
Good comments explain why, not just what — # calculate completion rate next to completion_rate <- completed / enrolled * 100 is less useful than # per supervisor's request, using enrollment totals as of this month's data pull, since the code itself already shows what is being calculated.

title: 7  Comments
text:
# This entire line is ignored by R
enrolled <- 130  # so is everything after the # on this line


Explaining the purpose of a script at the top (what problem does this solve?)
Explaining why a particular value or approach was chosen, when it isn’t obvious
Temporarily disabling a line of code without deleting it, by placing # in front of it

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.2 Demonstration
# completion_rate.R
# Purpose: Calculate the study completion rate for the monthly report
# Data source: Enrollment tracking spreadsheet, updated by the study coordinator
# Written by: [your name], [date]

enrolled <- 130    # total participants enrolled this month
completed <- 109   # participants who completed all required visits

# Completion rate as a percentage of enrolled participants
completion_rate <- completed / enrolled * 100
completion_rate
[1] 83.84615
Running this script produces exactly the same result as before — comments have zero effect on what R computes.

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.3 Explore
Try adding a # in front of the line completion_rate <- completed / enrolled * 100 and run the script. What happens to the final line, completion_rate, when that calculation never runs? Predict the error before you try it.

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.4 Solve the Problem
You rewrite completion_rate.R with clear comments before handing it to the new intern:
# completion_rate.R
# Purpose: Calculate the percentage of enrolled participants who completed the study
# Used for: Monthly report to the health department director
# Update instructions: replace 'enrolled' and 'completed' with this month's totals

enrolled <- 130    # total participants enrolled as of this month
completed <- 109   # participants who finished all study visits

completion_rate <- completed / enrolled * 100
completion_rate
Now the new intern can read the top three comment lines and immediately understand what the script is for, what to update, and where the numbers come from — without asking you a single question.

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.5 Interpret the Result
The calculation itself still returns 83.84615, unchanged. What changed is that the script is now self-documenting: anyone opening it — a new intern, a supervisor, or you in six months — can understand its purpose without additional explanation.

Common Mistakes

Over-commenting the obvious. x <- 5  # set x to 5 restates what’s already visible in the code and adds no value; better comments explain why 5 was chosen.
Under-commenting the non-obvious. Leaving a script with zero explanation for where data comes from or why a specific approach was used forces every future reader (including you) to reverse-engineer your intent.
Forgetting comments don’t need to “close.” Unlike some languages, R comments run from # to the end of that line only — there’s no special closing symbol, and a comment never spans multiple lines automatically.
Commenting out code and forgetting about it. A #-disabled line left in a script for months can confuse future readers about whether it’s meant to run or not — clean up disabled code once you’re sure you don’t need it.

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.6 Practice Problem
Take the dropout_rate.R script you wrote in the previous page’s practice problem and add: a purpose comment at the top, a comment explaining where the numbers came from, and inline comments on the enrolled and dropped lines.

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.7 Challenge Problem
Write a short script (3–4 lines of actual code) that calculates a body mass index (BMI) from a weight in kilograms and a height in meters, using the formula weight / height^2. Document it fully with comments so that someone with zero statistics background could still understand and reuse it.

title: 7  Comments
text:
7.8 Key Takeaways

Comments start with # and are completely ignored by R — they exist only for human readers.
Good comments explain why, not just what — the code itself already shows what’s happening.
# can also temporarily disable a line of code without deleting it.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What symbol starts a comment in R?

//
#
/* */
--

True or false. Comments affect how R executes a script.
Predict the output.
x <- 10  # setting x
# x <- 999
x
Write the code. Write a two-line script that stores the number of study sites (5) in an object called n_sites, with a comment explaining what the object represents.



Show answers


b) #
False. Comments are entirely ignored by R and have no effect on execution or results.
[1] 10 — the second line is commented out, so x is never reassigned to 999.
n_sites <- 5  # number of participating study sites
n_sites

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.1 The R Concept
R supports all standard arithmetic operators, and follows the usual mathematical order of operations (PEMDAS/BODMAS):
Parentheses () override the default order, exactly as in standard math notation. When in doubt, use them — they make your intent explicit even when they’re not strictly required.

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
Operator
Meaning
Example
Result




+
addition
5 + 3
8


-
subtraction
5 - 3
2


*
multiplication
5 * 3
15


/
division
5 / 3
1.6667


^
exponent (power)
5 ^ 2
25


%%
modulo (remainder)
5 %% 3
2


%/%
integer division
5 %/% 3
1

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.2 Demonstration
# Order of operations matters: multiplication happens before addition
2 + 3 * 4
[1] 14
# Parentheses force addition to happen first
(2 + 3) * 4
[1] 20
# Converting 154 pounds to kilograms
weight_lb <- 154
weight_kg <- weight_lb / 2.20462
weight_kg
[1] 69.85342
# BMI = weight (kg) / height (m) squared
height_m <- 1.75
bmi <- weight_kg / height_m^2
bmi
[1] 22.80887

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.3 Explore
Modify height_m to 1.60 and rerun the BMI calculation. Then try writing the BMI formula without parentheses around height_m^2 as weight_kg / height_m^2 versus incorrectly as (weight_kg / height_m)^2 — compare the two results and notice how differently they turn out.

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.4 Solve the Problem
For a patient weighing 180 pounds and standing 1.68 meters tall:
weight_lb <- 180
weight_kg <- weight_lb / 2.20462
height_m <- 1.68

bmi <- weight_kg / height_m^2
bmi
[1] 28.92108

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.5 Interpret the Result
A BMI of approximately 28.9 falls in the “overweight” category by standard WHO classification (25–29.9), which is the kind of clinical interpretation that would go directly into a patient chart or research dataset alongside this calculated value.

Common Mistakes

Misjudging order of operations. Writing weight_kg / height_m^2 relies on ^ happening before / — which R does correctly by default — but when unsure, add parentheses: weight_kg / (height_m^2).
Using x instead of * for multiplication. R has no concept of x as a multiplication symbol; only * works.
Confusing / and %/%. 7 / 2 gives 3.5 (regular division); 7 %/% 2 gives 3 (integer division, dropping the remainder). Using the wrong one silently produces a different kind of number.
Forgetting units before converting. A common real error is applying a kg formula to a still-in-pounds value, producing a BMI that looks plausible but is completely wrong.

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.6 Practice Problem
A dosing protocol calls for 5 mg of medication per kilogram of body weight. Calculate the correct total dose (in mg) for a patient weighing 198 pounds.

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.7 Challenge Problem
Body surface area (BSA) is sometimes estimated using the Mosteller formula: sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600). Using height_m <- 1.72 and weight_kg <- 75, calculate BSA — remembering to first convert height into centimeters (hint: sqrt() is a built-in function you can use directly, and you’ll learn more about functions like it later in this level).

title: 8  Basic Arithmetic
text:
8.8 Key Takeaways

R supports +, -, *, /, ^, %%, and %/%, following standard order of operations.
Parentheses () override default order and make calculations easier to read correctly.
Getting units right before applying a formula matters as much as getting the formula’s operators right.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does %% compute?

Percentage
Integer division
Remainder after division (modulo)
Square root

True or false. 2 + 3 * 4 and (2 + 3) * 4 give the same result in R.
Predict the output.
10 %% 3
Find the error. A student writes weight_lb x 2.20462 intending to convert kilograms to pounds. What’s wrong?



Show answers


c) Remainder after division (modulo).
False. 2 + 3 * 4 = 14 (multiplication first); (2 + 3) * 4 = 20 (parentheses force addition first).
[1] 1 (10 divided by 3 is 3 remainder 1).
R doesn’t recognize x as a multiplication symbol — it should be weight_lb * 2.20462.

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.1 The R Concept
The assignment operator, <-, stores a value under a name you choose:
Read <- as “gets” — bmi <- 22.8 means “bmi gets the value 22.8.” Once assigned, you can use bmi anywhere in your later code exactly as if you’d typed 22.8 directly.
R also accepts = for assignment in most contexts, and you’ll see both in other people’s code. This course uses <- throughout, which is the long-standing convention in the R community — it visually distinguishes “storing a value” from “specifying a function argument” (which uses =, as you’ll see in later lessons), a distinction that keeps longer scripts easier to read.
Keyboard shortcut: Alt + - (Windows/Linux) or Option + - (Mac) types <- automatically, including the spaces around it.
Nothing prints to the Console when you assign a value — assignment is a silent action. To see the value, type the object’s name on its own line afterward.

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text: name <- value

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.2 Demonstration
# Assign a value - nothing prints
bmi <- 22.8

# Now print it by typing its name alone
bmi
[1] 22.8
# Reuse the stored value in further calculations
is_high_bmi <- bmi >= 25
is_high_bmi
[1] FALSE
# Overwriting: assigning a new value to an existing name replaces it
bmi <- 27.4
bmi
[1] 27.4

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.3 Explore
Assign age <- 45, then create age_in_5_years <- age + 5 using the stored object rather than typing 45 again. Print both objects. Then reassign age <- 52 — does age_in_5_years automatically update? Predict the answer before running it.

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.4 Solve the Problem
weight_kg <- 78
height_m <- 1.70

bmi <- weight_kg / height_m^2
bmi
[1] 26.98962
# Reuse bmi for further checks, without retyping the formula
is_overweight <- bmi >= 25 & bmi < 30
is_overweight
[1] TRUE
# Reuse it again in a rounded summary
bmi_rounded <- round(bmi, 1)
bmi_rounded
[1] 27
The BMI value is now calculated once and reused three times, with zero risk of retyping it incorrectly.

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.5 Interpret the Result
Storing bmi once and reusing it means every downstream calculation — the overweight flag, the rounded summary — is guaranteed to be based on the exact same value, with no chance of a copy-paste typo silently introducing an inconsistency between them.

Common Mistakes

Expecting objects to update automatically. age_in_5_years does not recalculate when age changes later — it was computed once, at assignment time, using whatever age was at that moment. You’d need to rerun the calculation line to update it.
Forgetting to print after assigning. bmi <- 22.8 shows nothing in the Console; you must run bmi on its own line (or wrap the assignment in parentheses: (bmi <- 22.8)) to see the value.
Using < and - with a space between them. < - (with a space) is not the same as <- — R will likely produce an error or unexpected comparison. Use the keyboard shortcut to avoid this.
Mixing up <- direction. 5 <- bmi attempts to assign bmi’s value into 5, which is invalid — the name always goes on the left.

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.6 Practice Problem
Assign a patient’s systolic blood pressure of 142 to an object called sbp. Then create a second object, elevated_bp, that stores whether sbp is greater than or equal to 130 (a common elevated-BP threshold). Print both objects.

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.7 Challenge Problem
Assign enrolled <- 250 and completed <- 211. Using only these two objects (not the raw numbers again), calculate completion_rate as a percentage, then calculate dropout_rate as 100 - completion_rate, reusing completion_rate rather than recomputing it from scratch.

title: 9  The Assignment Operator
text:
9.8 Key Takeaways

<- assigns a value to a name; use the object’s name afterward to see or reuse it.
Assignment is silent — nothing prints unless you explicitly ask for the object by name.
Objects do not automatically update when the values they were built from change — they hold a fixed snapshot from the moment they were assigned.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does bmi <- 22.8 do?

Compares bmi to 22.8
Stores the value 22.8 under the name bmi
Prints 22.8 to the Console
Deletes the object bmi

True or false. If y <- x + 1 and then x is reassigned to a new value, y automatically updates too.
Predict the output.
score <- 88
score
Find the error.
90 <- passing_score



Show answers


b) Stores the value 22.8 under the name bmi.
False. y was calculated once, using x’s value at that moment — it does not recalculate automatically when x changes.
[1] 88
The assignment is backwards — the object name must be on the left of <-. It should be passing_score <- 90.

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.1 The R Concept
In R, virtually everything you create and store is called an object. A single number, a block of text, a list of a hundred ages, an entire imported spreadsheet — all of these become objects once assigned a name with <-. This is a deliberately broad, unifying idea: whether an object holds one value or a million, you interact with it the same way — by name.
Two ways to see what objects currently exist in your session:
To remove an object you no longer need:
And to clear everything at once (useful for starting fresh, though rarely needed mid-analysis):

title: 10  Objects
text:
The Environment pane (top-right in RStudio) — lists every object live, updating automatically as you create, modify, or remove them.
ls() — a function that lists all object names in your current session, directly in the Console.

ls()

rm(bmi_rounded)

rm(list = ls())

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.2 Demonstration
weight_kg <- 78
height_m <- 1.70
bmi <- weight_kg / height_m^2
is_overweight <- bmi >= 25 & bmi < 30
bmi_rounded <- round(bmi, 1)

# See every object currently in this session
ls()
[1] "bmi"           "bmi_rounded"   "height_m"      "is_overweight" "weight_kg"
Note that ls() returns names in alphabetical order, not the order you created them — useful to know so you don’t expect chronological order.

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.3 Explore
Run rm(bmi_rounded) and then ls() again. Confirm bmi_rounded is gone from the list, while the other four objects remain. Then try printing bmi_rounded directly — what error message does R give you?

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.4 Solve the Problem
ls()
[1] "bmi"           "bmi_rounded"   "height_m"      "is_overweight" "weight_kg"
You can now answer your supervisor precisely: “Right now, I have five objects stored — bmi, bmi_rounded, height_m, is_overweight, and weight_kg.” You didn’t need to scroll back through your whole script to answer confidently — ls() (or the Environment pane) gave you the live, current state directly.

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.5 Interpret the Result
The output of ls() is not the code that built these objects — it’s the current inventory of what those lines of code produced and left behind in memory. This is an important distinction: a script shows history; ls() and the Environment pane show present state.

Common Mistakes

Assuming the script and the Environment always match. If you delete a line from your script but already ran it, the object it created still exists in your session until you explicitly remove it or restart R.
Using an object that “shouldn’t” exist anymore. Because R doesn’t automatically clean up after you, leftover objects from earlier experimentation can accidentally get reused later in a script, producing confusing results.
Confusing rm() with deleting a file. rm(bmi) removes the object from your current session’s memory — it has no effect on any saved script or data file.

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.6 Practice Problem
Create three objects: n_enrolled <- 250, n_completed <- 211, and site_name <- "Riverdale Clinic". Run ls() and confirm all three appear. Then remove site_name with rm() and run ls() again to confirm it’s gone.

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.7 Challenge Problem
Without running any code, predict what ls() would return immediately after a fresh RStudio session starts, before you’ve created any objects. Then verify your prediction is correct — reason through what an empty environment should report.

title: 10  Objects
text:
10.8 Key Takeaways

Nearly everything you create in R — numbers, text, entire datasets — is stored as an object once assigned a name.
ls() and the Environment pane both show what currently exists in your session, which can differ from what your script currently contains.
rm() removes an object from memory but never touches any saved file.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does ls() show?

The lines of code in your current script
The names of all objects currently in your session
A list of installed packages
The contents of your working directory folder

True or false. Deleting a line of code from your script automatically removes the corresponding object from your session.
Predict the output.
a <- 1
b <- 2
rm(a)
ls()
Write the code. Write the command that removes every object currently in your session at once.



Show answers


b) The names of all objects currently in your session.
False. Removing a line from your script has no effect on objects already created in memory; you must explicitly run rm() or restart R to clear them.
[1] "b" — only b remains after a was removed.
rm(list = ls())

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.1 The R Concept
R’s actual naming rules (violating these causes an error) are:
Beyond the hard rules, there are style conventions — not enforced by R, but strongly recommended for readability:

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
Names can contain letters, numbers, dots (.), and underscores (_).
Names cannot start with a number (2nd_visit is invalid; second_visit is fine).
Names are case-sensitive: Age, age, and AGE are three different objects.
A handful of words are reserved by R itself (like TRUE, FALSE, if, for) and can’t be used as object names.



Use snake_case (lowercase words separated by underscores): bmi_rounded, is_overweight, n_enrolled. This is the convention used throughout the tidyverse and this course.
Make names descriptive but not excessively long: bmi is fine; body_mass_index_calculated_value is overkill.
Use consistent prefixes for related concepts: n_enrolled, n_completed (counts), is_overweight, is_smoker (logical flags).

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.2 Demonstration
# Poor naming - technically valid R, but unreadable
x <- 78
y <- 1.70
z <- x / y^2
temp2 <- z >= 25
# Clear naming - communicates meaning without needing comments
weight_kg <- 78
height_m <- 1.70
bmi <- weight_kg / height_m^2
is_overweight <- bmi >= 25
Both blocks compute exactly the same thing and produce identical results — the difference is entirely about how quickly a human reader understands them.
# This will cause an error - names can't start with a number
2nd_visit_bp <- 130
Error: unexpected symbol in "2nd_visit_bp"

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.3 Explore
Try creating an object called TRUE_value <- 5 — does it work? Then try creating one literally named TRUE <- 5 — what error do you get, and why do you think R protects this particular word?

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.4 Solve the Problem
Rewriting the inherited script with clear names:
weight_kg <- 78
height_m <- 1.70
bmi <- weight_kg / height_m^2
is_overweight <- bmi >= 25
Now any reader — including the next intern who inherits this script — can understand the purpose of every line without needing you to explain it in person.

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.5 Interpret the Result
The computed values are unchanged (bmi is still approximately 26.99, is_overweight is still TRUE), but the script itself has become documentation. Clear names reduce the need for comments explaining what a value represents, freeing comments to explain why, as discussed on the comments page.

Common Mistakes

Starting a name with a number. 1st_dose <- 50 fails; use first_dose or dose_1 instead.
Assuming names are case-insensitive. Age and age are two entirely separate objects in R — a frequent source of “object not found” errors when a name is typed with different capitalization than it was created with.
Using generic, uninformative names. data, df, temp, x1, x2 are common but tell a reader nothing about the actual content — reserve short generic names for genuinely temporary, throwaway calculations only.
Using reserved words. Trying to name an object TRUE, if, for, or function will fail, since these are reserved by R’s syntax itself.

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.6 Practice Problem
Rename the following poorly-named objects to follow this course’s snake_case convention, keeping their meaning clear: PatientAge, bp.systolic, SmokerFlag.

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.7 Challenge Problem
A colleague’s script uses df1, df2, and df3 for three different datasets: baseline enrollment, six-month follow-up, and twelve-month follow-up. Propose clearer names for all three, following the conventions on this page.

title: 11  Naming Objects
text:
11.8 Key Takeaways

R naming rules: letters, numbers, dots, and underscores are allowed; names can’t start with a number; names are case-sensitive.
This course (and the tidyverse broadly) uses snake_case: lowercase words joined with underscores.
Descriptive names reduce the need for comments and make scripts understandable at a glance.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. Which of these is a valid R object name?

2nd_dose
dose_2
dose-2
TRUE

True or false. Age and age refer to the same object in R.
Find the error.
1st_bp_reading <- 130
Write the code. Rename the object x to something descriptive, assuming it currently stores a participant’s diastolic blood pressure of 82.



Show answers


b) dose_2 — the others either start with a number, use an invalid hyphen, or are a reserved word.
False. R is case-sensitive; Age and age are two distinct objects.
Object names cannot start with a number. It should be bp_reading_1 <- 130 or similar.
diastolic_bp <- 82

title: 12  Basic Data Types
text:
12.1 The R Concept
Every value in R has a data type, and R treats different types differently, even when they look similar to a human. The three you’ll use constantly, starting today:
You can check any object’s type with class():
The error at the top of this page happened because age <- "34" created a character object — text that happens to look like a number — and R correctly refuses to perform arithmetic on text.

title: 12  Basic Data Types
text:
Numeric — actual numbers you can do math with: 34, 22.8, -5.
Character — text, always wrapped in quotes: "34", "Female", "Riverdale". Note that "34" is text, not a number, purely because of the quotes.
Logical — TRUE or FALSE values (no quotes), used for yes/no conditions.


class(34)      # numeric
class("34")    # character
class(TRUE)    # logical

title: 12  Basic Data Types
12  Basic Data Types
text:
12.3 Explore
Run class(age + 1) after setting age <- "34" and confirm you see the same error as in the opening problem. Then try class(as.numeric(age) + 1) — you’re using a conversion function here, which you’ll study properly on the numeric-data page next, but notice how wrapping age changes the outcome.

title: 12  Basic Data Types
text:
12.4 Solve the Problem
The fix is to make sure age is stored as a numeric value, not text:
age <- 34          # no quotes -> numeric
class(age)
[1] "numeric"
age_next_year <- age + 1
age_next_year
[1] 35

title: 12  Basic Data Types
text:
12.5 Interpret the Result
age_next_year now correctly equals 35, because age was stored as an actual number rather than a piece of text. The lesson generalizes: any time R refuses to do arithmetic you expect it to handle, class() is the first diagnostic tool to reach for.

Common Mistakes

Adding quotes around numbers by habit. age <- "34" looks harmless but silently creates text, not a number — a very common source of confusing downstream errors.
Assuming R will “figure out” what you meant. Unlike Excel, R does not automatically guess that "34" should behave like a number in a math context — it enforces the type strictly.
Forgetting quotes around genuine text. sex <- Female (no quotes) will error, because R interprets Female as an object name it can’t find, not as the text "Female".

title: 12  Basic Data Types
text:
12.6 Practice Problem
For each of the following, predict the result of class() before running it: class(120), class("120"), class(FALSE), class("TRUE").

title: 12  Basic Data Types
text:
12.7 Challenge Problem
A dataset column for blood type contains values like "O+", "AB-", "A+". Explain, in your own words, why this column should be stored as character data rather than numeric, even though blood types are sometimes informally coded with numbers elsewhere.

title: 12  Basic Data Types
text:
12.8 Key Takeaways

Every R value has a type; the three fundamentals are numeric, character, and logical.
class() reveals an object’s current type — your first stop when arithmetic or comparisons behave unexpectedly.
Quotes make the difference between a number (34) and text that merely looks like a number ("34").


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What is the class of "25" (with quotes)?

numeric
character
logical
integer

True or false. R will automatically convert "25" to a number if you try to do math with it.
Find the error.
n_visits <- "4"
total_visits <- n_visits + 2
Predict the output.
class(TRUE)



Show answers


b) character — the quotes make it text, regardless of what it looks like.
False. R will raise an error (non-numeric argument to binary operator) rather than silently converting it.
n_visits is stored as character text ("4"), not a number, so + 2 fails. Fix: n_visits <- 4 (no quotes).
[1] "logical"

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.1 The R Concept
R’s numeric type covers ordinary decimal numbers — what R internally calls “double precision” floating point numbers, which is more than enough precision for virtually all health and research data. (R also has a stricter “integer” type, marked with an L suffix like 5L, but you will rarely need to use it directly as a beginner — numeric handles both whole numbers and decimals fine.)
Two functions matter here:

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
is.numeric(x) — returns TRUE or FALSE, directly answering “is this numeric?”
as.numeric(x) — attempts to convert x into a numeric value. If x is text that clearly represents a number (like "45"), this works cleanly. If the text isn’t a valid number (like "forty-five"), R will produce NA and a warning, rather than crashing.

is.numeric(45)      # TRUE
is.numeric("45")    # FALSE - it's character, even though it looks numeric
as.numeric("45")    # converts "45" into the number 45

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.2 Demonstration
age_text <- "45"
is.numeric(age_text)
[1] FALSE
age_numeric <- as.numeric(age_text)
age_numeric
[1] 45
is.numeric(age_numeric)
[1] TRUE
# What happens with genuinely invalid text
as.numeric("forty-five")
[1] NA
Warning message:
NAs introduced by coercion

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.3 Explore
Try as.numeric("45 ") (with a trailing space, matching the opening problem) — does the extra space cause a problem, or does R handle it gracefully? Then try as.numeric("$45") — does a dollar sign behave the same way?

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.4 Solve the Problem
age_raw <- "45 "   # as it came in from the spreadsheet, with a trailing space
is.numeric(age_raw)
[1] FALSE
age_fixed <- as.numeric(age_raw)
age_fixed
[1] 45
is.numeric(age_fixed)
[1] TRUE
R’s as.numeric() handles the trailing space gracefully in this case, correctly converting "45 " into the number 45. The fix is a single line, run once the problem is identified.

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.5 Interpret the Result
The participant’s age is now correctly stored as the number 45, ready to be used in any calculation (like computing the sample’s mean age) rather than being silently excluded or causing errors because it was still text.

Common Mistakes

Assuming as.numeric() always succeeds silently. If the text isn’t a valid number (extra letters, stray symbols like currency signs), as.numeric() returns NA with a warning rather than an error — easy to miss if you’re not watching for warnings.
Not checking before converting an entire column. Running as.numeric() on a whole column of messy real-world data can silently turn several entries into NA if even a few values aren’t clean numbers — always check the result afterward.
Confusing is.numeric() with as.numeric(). One checks (is.), the other converts (as.) — mixing them up is a common beginner slip, since they sound similar.

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.6 Practice Problem
You have bp_text <- "128". Confirm with is.numeric() that it’s currently not numeric, convert it with as.numeric(), and confirm the conversion worked.

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.7 Challenge Problem
A column of self-reported weights includes the entries "70", "68.5", "unknown", and "72 kg". Predict what as.numeric() would produce for each of these four values individually, and explain why two of them would likely become NA.

title: 13  Numeric Data
text:
13.8 Key Takeaways

is.numeric() checks whether a value is numeric; as.numeric() attempts to convert a value into a number.
as.numeric() can gracefully handle some messiness (like stray whitespace) but returns NA with a warning for text that isn’t a valid number.
Always check the result after converting real-world data — silent NAs from a failed conversion are easy to miss.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does as.numeric("hello") return?

An error that stops your script
0
NA, with a warning
The text "hello" unchanged

True or false. is.numeric("45") returns TRUE.
Predict the output.
as.numeric("100")
Write the code. Convert dose_text <- "250" into a numeric object called dose_numeric, then confirm it’s numeric.



Show answers


c) NA, with a warning (“NAs introduced by coercion”) — R does not stop your script, but flags the failed conversion.
False. "45" is character data (it’s quoted); is.numeric() returns FALSE regardless of what the text looks like.
[1] 100
dose_numeric <- as.numeric(dose_text)
is.numeric(dose_numeric)

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.1 The R Concept
Character data is text, and it must always be wrapped in quotes — either single ('text') or double ("text"); this course uses double quotes consistently. Without quotes, R assumes you’re referring to an object name, not literal text — which is exactly why county <- Riverdale failed: R went looking for an existing object called Riverdale and found none.
Useful functions for working with character data:

title: 14  Character Data
text:
nchar(x) — counts the number of characters in a string.
paste(...) — combines multiple pieces of text (and even numbers) into one string, separated by a space by default.
paste0(...) — same as paste(), but with no separator between pieces — handy when you want text glued together with no gap.

nchar("Riverdale")             # 9
paste("Participant from", "Riverdale")   # "Participant from Riverdale"
paste0("ID_", 42)              # "ID_42"

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.2 Demonstration
county <- "Riverdale"
class(county)
[1] "character"
nchar(county)
[1] 9
label <- paste("Participant from", county)
label
[1] "Participant from Riverdale"
participant_id <- paste0("ID_", 42)
participant_id
[1] "ID_42"

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.3 Explore
Try paste("Participant from", county, sep = "-") — the sep argument changes the default separator. What does the output look like now? Then try combining three pieces of text at once: paste("Age:", 45, "County:", county).

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.4 Solve the Problem
county <- "Riverdale"   # quotes required - this is text, not an object reference
label <- paste("Participant from", county)
label
[1] "Participant from Riverdale"
The original error is fixed simply by adding quotes: R now correctly stores the literal text "Riverdale" rather than searching for a nonexistent object with that name.

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.5 Interpret the Result
label now holds a complete, human-readable sentence built by combining fixed text ("Participant from") with a stored value (county) — exactly the kind of thing you’d use to generate personalized summaries or labels across many participants at once (a technique you’ll extend to entire columns of data later in the course).

Common Mistakes

Forgetting quotes around text. county <- Riverdale causes an “object not found” error, since R interprets unquoted text as an object reference, not literal text.
Mixing up "TRUE" and TRUE. "TRUE" (quoted) is character data — just the text “TRUE” — while TRUE (unquoted) is logical data. They look similar but behave very differently.
Expecting paste() to add punctuation automatically. paste("Age:", 45) produces "Age: 45" with the default single-space separator — if you want something else (a comma, no space), you need the sep argument.
Confusing paste() and paste0(). paste("ID_", 42) produces "ID_ 42" (note the space), while paste0("ID_", 42) produces "ID_42" — a small but common source of unwanted spaces in generated labels.

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.6 Practice Problem
Create an object city <- "Oakford". Use paste0() to build a string reading exactly "Site: Oakford" (no extra space after the colon).

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.7 Challenge Problem
You have first_name <- "Amara" and last_name <- "Okafor". Build a single string full_id that reads "Okafor, Amara" using paste() with an appropriate separator.

title: 14  Character Data
text:
14.8 Key Takeaways

Character data must be quoted; unquoted text is interpreted as an object name and will error if that name doesn’t exist.
paste() combines text with a space separator by default (customizable via sep); paste0() combines text with no separator.
nchar() counts the characters in a string — useful for spotting unexpectedly long or short text entries during data checks.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. Which of these correctly stores the text “Female”?

sex <- Female
sex <- "Female"
sex = Female
sex <- Female()

True or false. paste0() adds a space between combined pieces of text by default.
Predict the output.
paste0("Visit_", 3)
Find the error.
status <- Active



Show answers


b) sex <- "Female" — quotes are required for text.
False. paste0() uses no separator by default; paste() uses a single space by default.
[1] "Visit_3"
Active is unquoted, so R looks for an object named Active and fails. It should be status <- "Active".

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.1 The R Concept
Logical data has exactly two possible values: TRUE or FALSE (always written in capital letters, never quoted — "TRUE" is character text, not logical). Logical values are most often produced by comparison operators:
You can also combine logical values using:
The single most common beginner mistake is confusing = (assignment) with == (equality comparison) — they look almost identical but do fundamentally different things.

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
Operator
Meaning




==
equal to


!=
not equal to


>
greater than


<
less than


>=
greater than or equal to


<=
less than or equal to







Operator
Meaning




&
and (both must be TRUE)


\|
or (at least one must be TRUE)


!
not (flips TRUE to FALSE, and vice versa)

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.2 Demonstration
age <- 68
is_senior <- age >= 65
is_senior
[1] TRUE
smoker_status <- "Yes"
is_smoker <- smoker_status == "Yes"
is_smoker
[1] TRUE
# Combining two conditions with &
high_risk <- is_senior & is_smoker
high_risk
[1] TRUE
# Flipping a logical value with !
is_nonsmoker <- !is_smoker
is_nonsmoker
[1] FALSE

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.3 Explore
Change age to 50 and rerun is_senior <- age >= 65. What do you get, and does high_risk change automatically? (Recall from the objects page: it won’t, unless you rerun the calculation.) Try age == "68" (comparing a number to quoted text) — does it still work as expected?

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.4 Solve the Problem
age <- 71
smoker_status <- "Yes"

is_senior <- age >= 65
is_smoker <- smoker_status == "Yes"

is_senior
[1] TRUE
is_smoker
[1] TRUE
Both flags now clearly and unambiguously answer your supervisor’s two questions for this participant, ready to be reused later (for example, to count or filter many participants at once — a technique covered in Level 5).

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.5 Interpret the Result
is_senior being TRUE means this participant meets the “65 or older” criterion; is_smoker being TRUE means they reported current smoking. Stored as logical values rather than text, these flags can be directly combined (as with high_risk above) or later used to filter a whole dataset down to exactly the participants who match both conditions.

Common Mistakes

Confusing = and ==. age = 65 assigns 65 to age; age == 65 checks whether age equals 65. Using the wrong one produces very different, sometimes silently wrong, results.
Quoting TRUE/FALSE unnecessarily. is_smoker <- "TRUE" creates character text, not a logical value — drop the quotes: is_smoker <- TRUE.
Misusing & versus |. age >= 65 & age <= 75 requires both conditions; using | instead would match ages 65 and under or 75 and over — the opposite of what’s usually intended for an age range.
Forgetting operator precedence. Comparisons are evaluated before &/|, but when combining several conditions, parentheses make your intent explicit and prevent subtle bugs.

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.6 Practice Problem
A participant has systolic_bp <- 145. Create a logical object has_elevated_bp that is TRUE if systolic_bp is greater than or equal to 130.

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.7 Challenge Problem
A participant has age <- 58 and bmi <- 31.2. Create a logical object needs_followup that is TRUE only if the participant is both under 65 and has a BMI of 30 or above (using &), then create a second object either_condition using | instead, and explain in one sentence why the two results differ.

title: 15  Logical Data
text:
15.8 Key Takeaways

Logical values are TRUE or FALSE (unquoted); comparison operators (==, !=, >, <, >=, <=) produce them.
= assigns a value; == checks equality — mixing these up is one of the most common beginner errors.
& (and), | (or), and ! (not) combine or invert logical values, and parentheses help make combined conditions unambiguous.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does age == 65 do?

Assigns 65 to age
Checks whether age equals 65, returning TRUE or FALSE
Rounds age to 65
Creates a character string

True or false. "TRUE" and TRUE are the same value in R.
Predict the output.
x <- 10
y <- 20
(x < y) & (y < 30)
Find the error.
is_adult <- age = 18



Show answers


b) Checks whether age equals 65, returning TRUE or FALSE.
False. "TRUE" (quoted) is character text; TRUE (unquoted) is logical — they have different classes.
[1] TRUE — both 10 < 20 and 20 < 30 are true, and & requires both.
The code uses = where == was intended for comparison (and the logic is also reversed). It likely should read is_adult <- age >= 18.

title: 16  Missing Values
text:
16.1 The R Concept
R represents a missing value with the special symbol NA (no quotes — it’s not the text “NA”, it’s a distinct, reserved value meaning “this data point does not exist”). NA can appear inside numeric, character, or logical data alike.
Critically, NA is contagious in calculations: almost any arithmetic involving an NA produces NA as the result, because R has no way to know what the missing value “would have been.”
To detect missing values, use is.na(x) — never x == NA, which will not work the way you’d expect (comparing anything to NA, including NA itself, returns NA, not TRUE).

title: 16  Missing Values
text:
5 + NA     # NA - unknown plus 5 is still unknown
mean(c(20, 30, NA))    # NA - one unknown value makes the whole average unknown, by default

is.na(NA)    # TRUE
NA == NA     # NA, not TRUE - this is a common trap

title: 16  Missing Values
text:
16.2 Demonstration
age <- NA
age
[1] NA
is.na(age)
[1] TRUE
# NA is contagious in arithmetic
age + 1
[1] NA
# The trap: don't use == to check for NA
age == NA
[1] NA
ages <- c(45, 62, NA, 38, 29)
mean(ages)
[1] NA

title: 16  Missing Values
text:
16.3 Explore
Run sum(is.na(ages)) on the vector above — what do you think this counts? (Hint: is.na() produces TRUE/FALSE for each element, and TRUE behaves like 1 when summed.) Then try mean(ages, na.rm = TRUE) — you’ll study na.rm properly on the very next page, but see what changes.

title: 16  Missing Values
text:
16.4 Solve the Problem
ages <- c(45, 62, NA, 38, 29, NA, 51, NA, 67, 33)   # 3 missing, matching the problem

# How many are missing?
sum(is.na(ages))
[1] 3
# The naive average fails, exactly as predicted
mean(ages)
[1] NA
You’ve now correctly identified that three participants have missing ages, and confirmed that a plain mean() call refuses to guess an answer around them — which is the right, safe default behavior. The actual fix for computing a usable average despite the missing values is covered on the very next page, using mean()’s na.rm argument.

title: 16  Missing Values
text:
16.5 Interpret the Result
sum(is.na(ages)) returning 3 confirms exactly three participants have missing age data, matching what your supervisor described. mean(ages) returning NA is not a bug — it’s R correctly refusing to compute an average when it doesn’t have complete information, forcing you to make an explicit, informed decision about how to handle the gap.

Common Mistakes

Treating missing as zero. A missing age is not the same as an age of 0 — silently substituting zero would badly distort any average or model built on that variable.
Using == NA instead of is.na(). age == NA always returns NA, never TRUE, even when age genuinely is NA — this is one of the most common and confusing beginner traps in R.
Assuming NA always causes an error. It doesn’t — R happily computes with NA and simply propagates it forward as an “unknown” result, which can silently hide a missing-data problem if you’re not specifically checking with is.na().
