Try pct_missing(participants$diastolic_bp). Confirm it correctly returns 0, exactly matching your hand calculation from the previous page’s practice problem — your function works on any vector passed to it, not just the ones you tested first.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.4 Solve the Problem
pct_missing <- function(x) {
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

pct_missing(participants$id)
[1] 0
pct_missing(participants$smoker)
[1] 0
pct_missing(participants$county)
[1] 0
id, smoker, and county all correctly return 0 — none of these columns have any missing values, and pct_missing() handles them exactly as correctly as it handled the columns that did have missing data, without needing any special-casing.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.5 Interpret the Result
pct_missing() now works as a genuine, general-purpose tool — callable on any column in participants, or in any other dataset entirely, without ever needing to see or modify its internal code again. This is the core payoff of writing a function: the calculation was written once, correctly, and now every future use of it is just a short function call.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting the curly braces {} for multi-line functions. A single-line function body can skip the braces (pct_missing <- function(x) sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100), but any function with more than one line needs {} to group them together.
Assuming a function needs an explicit return() to produce output. R automatically returns the last evaluated expression — return() is optional and mainly useful for exiting a function early (covered later in this level) or for clarity in longer functions.
Confusing the argument name (x) with any specific column. x inside the function body is just a placeholder name — it takes on whatever value is passed in when the function is called (participants$age, participants$bmi, or anything else), and has no meaning outside the function itself.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.6 Practice Problem
Write a function called range_width that takes a numeric vector x and returns the difference between its maximum and minimum values (using na.rm = TRUE inside), then test it on participants$bmi.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.7 Challenge Problem
Write a function called mean_and_sd that takes a numeric vector x and returns both the mean and standard deviation as a named vector (hint: c(mean = ..., sd = ...)), handling missing values appropriately. Test it on participants$age and confirm both values match Level 7’s earlier findings (mean 47.43, SD 15.03).

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.8 Key Takeaways

A function is created with function(arguments) { code }, and is called later by name with actual values in place of its arguments.
R automatically returns the last evaluated expression from a function — an explicit return() is optional, not required.
Once written and tested, a function becomes a reusable tool that works on any appropriate input, not just the specific examples used while building it.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does R return from a function by default, with no explicit return()?

Nothing — an error occurs
The first evaluated expression
The last evaluated expression
NULL, always

True or false. Curly braces {} are required for every function, even one-line ones.
Predict the output. Given pct_missing <- function(x) sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100, what would pct_missing(c(1, NA, 3, NA)) return?
Find the error.
pct_missing <- function(x)
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
  cat("Done!")



Show answers


c) The last evaluated expression.
False. A single-line function body can omit the curly braces; they’re only required when the body spans multiple lines that need to be grouped together.
50 — 2 of the 4 values are NA, so 2 / 4 * 100 = 50.
Without curly braces, only the very next single line (sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100) is treated as the function’s body — the cat("Done!") line runs separately, outside the function, every time this code is sourced, rather than being part of pct_missing()’s behavior. Wrapping both lines in {} would fix this ambiguity.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.1 The R Concept
A default argument value is specified with = directly in the function definition, and is used automatically whenever that argument is omitted from the call:
Calling describe_numeric(participants$age) uses digits = 2 automatically; calling describe_numeric(participants$age, digits = 0) overrides it explicitly. This is exactly the same pattern behind mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)’s na.rm argument, which defaults to FALSE unless you explicitly set it to TRUE.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.2 Demonstration
describe_numeric <- function(x, digits = 2) {
  c(
    mean = round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits),
    sd = round(sd(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits)
  )
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

describe_numeric(participants$age)
 mean    sd
47.43 15.03
describe_numeric(participants$age, digits = 0)
mean   sd
  47   15
Calling the function with no digits argument at all uses the default (2 decimal places); explicitly passing digits = 0 overrides that default, rounding to whole numbers instead.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.3 Explore
Try describe_numeric(participants$bmi, digits = 4). Confirm the function happily accepts any value for digits, not just 0 or 2 — the default is only ever a fallback, never a restriction on what values are allowed.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.4 Solve the Problem
describe_numeric <- function(x, digits = 2) {
  c(
    mean = round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits),
    sd = round(sd(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits)
  )
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

describe_numeric(participants$systolic_bp)
  mean     sd
129.84  18.87
describe_numeric(participants$systolic_bp, digits = 1)
 mean    sd
129.8  18.9
Both calls compute the exact same underlying mean and SD — only the rounding precision changes, controlled entirely by whether digits is left at its default (2) or explicitly overridden (1).

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.5 Interpret the Result
describe_numeric() now serves both the quick-internal-check case (default 2 decimals) and the more precise or more rounded reporting case (any digits value explicitly requested), without needing two separate functions or repeating the round()-wrapped calculation twice. A well-chosen default lets the common case stay simple, while still leaving full flexibility available when needed.

Common Mistakes

Choosing a default that silently produces wrong-looking results for typical use. A poorly chosen default (like digits = 10) could produce overly precise, hard-to-read output for the common case — defaults should reflect what’s usually wanted, not just any arbitrary value.
Forgetting default arguments can reference other arguments. More advanced function definitions can have one default depend on another argument’s value — while not needed here, it’s worth knowing defaults aren’t limited to fixed constants.
Assuming a default value means an argument can never be changed. A default is only what’s used if omitted — every default-valued argument remains fully overridable at the calling site, exactly as shown with digits = 0 and digits = 1 above.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.6 Practice Problem
Modify pct_missing() from Page 2 to accept a digits argument with a default of 1, rounding its result before returning it. Test it on participants$age both with and without specifying digits.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.7 Challenge Problem
Write a function called flag_high_bp that takes a numeric vector of systolic blood pressure values and a threshold argument defaulting to 130 (Level 4’s hypertension cutoff), returning TRUE/FALSE for each value indicating whether it exceeds the threshold. Test it once with the default threshold, and once with threshold = 140 (a stricter cutoff), and compare how many values are flagged under each.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.8 Key Takeaways

A default argument value (argument = default_value in the function definition) is used automatically whenever that argument is omitted from a call.
Defaults make the common case simple to call, while every default remains fully overridable when a different value is needed.
This is the exact same pattern behind built-in functions like mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), whose na.rm argument defaults to FALSE.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In function(x, digits = 2), when is digits equal to 2?

Always, regardless of how the function is called
Only when the caller explicitly writes digits = 2
Whenever the caller omits the digits argument entirely
Never — defaults are just documentation, not actual behavior

True or false. A default argument value can always be overridden by the caller.
Predict the output. Given describe_numeric <- function(x, digits = 2) {...}, what would digits equal inside a call to describe_numeric(participants$bmi)?
Find the error.
describe_numeric <- function(x, digits) {
  c(mean = round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits))
}
describe_numeric(participants$age)



Show answers


c) Whenever the caller omits the digits argument entirely.
True. A default is only ever a fallback for when an argument is omitted — it never prevents the caller from specifying a different value.
2 — the default value, since the call didn’t specify digits.
digits has no default value here, so calling describe_numeric(participants$age) without specifying digits produces an error (argument "digits" is missing, with no default). Adding digits = 2 (or any default) to the function definition would fix this.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.1 The R Concept
A function’s body can contain the exact same if/else if/else logic (Level 4) you’d use outside a function — the only difference is that the result becomes the function’s return value:
Whichever branch actually runs becomes the function’s return value automatically — R returns the last evaluated expression, exactly as on Page 2, and here that last expression differs depending on which if/else if branch was taken.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.3 Explore
Try bp_category(120) and bp_category(129) — both should return "Elevated", since the boundaries use < (strictly less than), meaning the lower bound of each category belongs to that category, not the one before it. Confirm this matches Level 4’s case_when() boundary logic exactly.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.5 Interpret the Result
bp_category() reproduces Level 4’s classification logic exactly, but packaged as a reusable tool that works on one value at a time — useful in contexts where you don’t want to build (or don’t have) a full data frame, like classifying a single new reading as it comes in. The matching counts (18/32/17/29) confirm the function was written correctly, cross-checked against a completely different implementation from an earlier level.

Common Mistakes

Getting boundary conditions backwards inside a function, same as in Level 4. sbp < 120 versus sbp <= 120 produces different classifications right at the boundary value 120 — the same care needed for case_when() conditions applies identically inside a function’s if statements.
Forgetting else needs to handle every remaining case. If the final else branch were missing, a systolic BP of 150 (falling through every if/else if condition) would return NULL instead of a category — always ensure the final else genuinely catches everything not handled above it.
Passing a vector to a function written for a single value. bp_category() as written expects one number at a time — passing an entire column directly (bp_category(participants$systolic_bp)) doesn’t work as intended, because if in R expects a single logical value, not a vector; sapply() (Page 8) is what applies a single-value function across an entire column correctly.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.6 Practice Problem
Write a function called age_group that takes a single age value and returns "Senior" for ages 65 and older, "Adult" for ages 18-64, using if/else. Test it on a few individual values.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.7 Challenge Problem
Modify bp_category() to also handle a "Low" category for sbp < 90 (add this as the very first condition checked), and re-run it across participants$systolic_bp. Given the minimum recorded systolic BP is exactly 90, would you expect this new category to ever actually appear in the results?

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.8 Key Takeaways

A function’s body can contain the same if/else if/else logic used outside a function (Level 4) — whichever branch runs becomes the function’s automatic return value.
Boundary conditions (< versus <=) require the same care inside a function as they do in any standalone conditional.
Cross-checking a function’s output against a previously verified result (like Level 4’s case_when() counts) is a reliable way to confirm the function was written correctly.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In bp_category(), what does the function return for sbp = 135?

"Normal"
"Elevated"
"Stage 1"
"Stage 2"

True or false. A missing final else branch could cause a function to return NULL for values not covered by any earlier condition.
Predict the output. What would bp_category(119) and bp_category(120) each return?
Short answer. Why does cross-checking bp_category()’s counts (18/32/17/29) against Level 4’s case_when() counts provide good evidence the function is correct?



Show answers


c) "Stage 1" — 135 is at least 130 but less than 140.
True. If no if/else if condition matches and there’s no final else, the function returns NULL for that input, rather than a meaningful category.
bp_category(119) returns "Normal" (since 119 < 120); bp_category(120) returns "Elevated" (since 120 is not less than 120, so it falls to the next condition).
Two independently written implementations (Level 4’s case_when() inside mutate(), and this level’s if/else if function) producing identical counts on the same data is strong evidence neither has a logic error — if either had a boundary mistake, the two counts would very likely disagree.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.1 The R Concept
A function can accept as many arguments as it needs, listed in order, separated by commas:
Notice is_at_risk() combines two required arguments (age, sbp) with two optional, default-valued arguments (age_cutoff, bp_cutoff, Page 3) — a function can freely mix both kinds.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.2 Demonstration
is_at_risk <- function(age, sbp, age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130) {
  age >= age_cutoff & sbp >= bp_cutoff
}

is_at_risk(70, 145)
[1] TRUE
is_at_risk(50, 145)
[1] FALSE
The first participant (age 70, BP 145) meets both thresholds and is flagged TRUE; the second (age 50, BP 145) fails the age condition alone, so the combined result is FALSE — exactly the & (“and”) logic from Level 1, now packaged inside a reusable function.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.3 Explore
Try is_at_risk(70, 145, age_cutoff = 75). Since 70 no longer meets a stricter age cutoff of 75, this should return FALSE — confirming the default-valued arguments (Page 3) remain fully overridable even in a function with multiple parameters.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.4 Solve the Problem
is_at_risk <- function(age, sbp, age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130) {
  age >= age_cutoff & sbp >= bp_cutoff
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

at_risk_flags <- is_at_risk(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp)

sum(at_risk_flags, na.rm = TRUE)
[1] 4
Because age >= age_cutoff & sbp >= bp_cutoff uses R’s vectorized & operator (Level 2), is_at_risk() works correctly whether called on two single numbers or two entire columns at once — no sapply() or loop required here, since & already compares element-by-element automatically. The result (4 at-risk participants) exactly matches Level 5’s filter(age >= 65 & systolic_bp >= 130) count.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.5 Interpret the Result
is_at_risk() reproduces Level 5’s flagship finding (4 at-risk participants) exactly, but as a named, reusable, customizable tool — the age and blood pressure cutoffs can be adjusted per call without editing the function’s internals, unlike Level 5’s filter() call, which would need its condition rewritten by hand for a different threshold.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting argument order matters when calling a function positionally. is_at_risk(145, 70) (blood pressure first, age second) silently swaps the two values’ roles — always double check argument order, or use named arguments (is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145)) to avoid ambiguity entirely.
Assuming every function automatically vectorizes like is_at_risk() did here. This function happened to work correctly on whole columns because &, >= are already vectorized operators (Level 2) — not every function behaves this way automatically, especially ones containing if (Page 4), which expects a single value, not a vector.
Mixing required and default arguments in the wrong order. In R, arguments with defaults should generally come after arguments without defaults in the function definition — function(age_cutoff = 65, age, sbp) would still technically work when called with named arguments, but is much more error-prone and confusing to read.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.6 Practice Problem
Write a function called is_high_risk_smoker that takes bmi, smoker, and a bmi_cutoff defaulting to 30, returning TRUE if BMI meets or exceeds the cutoff and the participant is a current smoker. Test it on the full participants data frame and report how many are flagged.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.7 Challenge Problem
Modify is_at_risk() to accept a third required argument, smoker, and flag a participant only if all three conditions hold: age 65+, systolic BP 130+, and current smoker status. How many participants meet this stricter combined definition, compared to the original 4?

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.8 Key Takeaways

A function can take multiple arguments, listed in order and separated by commas, freely mixing required and default-valued arguments.
Whether a function automatically works on whole vectors depends on whether its internal operations (like &, >=) are already vectorized — this isn’t automatic for every function.
Named arguments (is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145)) avoid the ambiguity of positional argument order, especially as a function’s argument count grows.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In is_at_risk(age, sbp, age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130), which arguments are optional?

age and sbp
age_cutoff and bp_cutoff
All four are required
None — all four have defaults

True or false. Calling is_at_risk(145, 70) (with arguments in the wrong order) will produce an error rather than a silently wrong answer.
Predict the output. What would is_at_risk(80, 150, age_cutoff = 70, bp_cutoff = 140) return?
Find the error.
is_at_risk(sbp = 70, 145)
# intended: age = 70, sbp = 145



Show answers


b) age_cutoff and bp_cutoff — both have default values, making them optional.
False. R doesn’t detect that the arguments are “backwards” — it silently treats the first positional value as age and the second as sbp, producing a plausible-looking but incorrect result rather than an error.
TRUE — age 80 ≥ 70 and blood pressure 150 ≥ 140, so both conditions hold.
sbp was named explicitly as 70, so the remaining positional value 145 fills the next unfilled argument, age — meaning this call actually evaluates as age = 145, sbp = 70, the reverse of what was intended. Mixing named and positional arguments is legal in R, but it’s easy to misread; naming both arguments explicitly (is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145)) avoids this kind of silent mix-up entirely.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.1 The R Concept
Every function call creates its own local scope — a private workspace for variables created or received as arguments inside that function. Variables inside a function’s local scope:

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
Are isolated from your main (“global”) workspace — a variable named age inside a function has no connection to any age object outside it, even if they share the exact same name.
Disappear once the function finishes running — they exist only for the duration of that one function call.
Can read variables from the global environment if not shadowed by a local one of the same name, but cannot modify the global version by simply assigning to a same-named local variable.

age <- 100   # global variable

check_age <- function(age) {
  age <- age + 1   # this only changes the LOCAL copy of age
  age
}

check_age(30)   # returns 31
age             # still 100 - completely unaffected

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.2 Demonstration
age <- 100

check_age <- function(age) {
  age <- age + 1
  age
}

check_age(30)
[1] 31
age
[1] 100
Even though the function’s argument is also named age, and the function reassigns age internally, the global age (100) is completely untouched — the function’s age was always a separate, local copy that ceased to exist the moment the function finished.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.3 Explore
Try defining local_total <- 0 inside a function body, running the function, and then checking whether local_total exists in your main environment afterward (it won’t — exists("local_total") will return FALSE). Variables created inside a function’s body are just as local as its arguments.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.4 Solve the Problem
pct_missing <- function(x) {
  n_missing <- sum(is.na(x))       # local variable, exists only inside this call
  n_total <- length(x)             # also local
  n_missing / n_total * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

pct_missing(participants$age)
[1] 6
exists("n_missing")
[1] FALSE
n_missing and n_total were created and used entirely inside pct_missing()’s local scope — after the function call finishes, exists("n_missing") confirms they never leaked into your main workspace at all.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.5 Interpret the Result
This isolation is a genuine safety feature, not a limitation: it means you can freely reuse short, convenient variable names like x, n, or age inside any function without ever worrying about accidentally overwriting an important variable of the same name in your main script. Functions act as self-contained units — what happens inside, stays inside, unless you explicitly return a value out.

Common Mistakes

Expecting a function to modify a global variable by assigning to a same-named local one. check_age()’s internal age <- age + 1 only ever changes the local copy — to actually update something in your main workspace, the function would need to return() the new value and you’d need to reassign it yourself (age <- check_age(age)).
Assuming variables created inside a function are available for debugging afterward. If a function errors partway through, its local variables (like n_missing above) are simply gone — this is why printing intermediate values during development (before wrapping code in a function) is often easier for debugging than trying to inspect a function’s internals after the fact.
Reusing a global variable’s exact name as a function argument and assuming this causes a conflict. As shown above, this is actually completely safe — R always prioritizes the local (argument) version inside the function body, with no naming conflict at all.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.6 Practice Problem
Write a small function that takes an argument called bmi, adds 1 to it internally, and returns the result. Call it with participants$bmi[1] as the argument, then confirm that participants$bmi[1] itself is completely unchanged afterward.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.7 Challenge Problem
Predict, without running any code, what this snippet prints for each line:
total <- 10
add_five <- function(total) {
  total <- total + 5
  total
}
print(add_five(total))
print(total)
Then run it to confirm your prediction.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.8 Key Takeaways

Every function call creates its own local scope — variables inside it (arguments and internally created ones alike) are isolated from your main workspace.
A function can read global variables it hasn’t shadowed, but assigning to a same-named local variable never modifies the global version.
Local variables disappear once a function finishes running, which is a safety feature protecting your main workspace from accidental modification.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. After check_age(30) runs (as defined above), what is the value of the global age?

31
30
100 (unchanged)
NULL

True or false. Variables created inside a function’s body continue to exist in your main workspace after the function finishes running.
Predict the output. If a function argument is named x, and your main workspace also has a variable called x, does the function’s internal use of x affect the global one?
Short answer. Why is it considered safe practice to reuse short variable names like x or n inside many different functions?



Show answers


c) 100 — the global age is completely unaffected by the function’s local reassignment.
False. Local variables exist only for the duration of the function call and disappear once it finishes.
No — the function’s x is entirely local and isolated from any global variable of the same name, unless the function explicitly returns a value that you then reassign to the global x yourself.
Because each function call creates its own isolated local scope, reusing a name like x in one function has no effect on any variable of the same name used elsewhere, including in your main workspace or inside other functions.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.1 The R Concept
A function doesn’t need to be assigned a name to be used immediately — this is called an anonymous function:
R also provides a shorter syntax for anonymous functions, using a backslash \ instead of the full word function:
Both versions define the exact same function — the \(x) shorthand is simply less typing, and is especially common when passing a quick function directly into another function (like sapply(), previewed next page), rather than defining it separately first.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
function(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)

\(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.2 Demonstration
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Anonymous function, called immediately - no name assigned
(function(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))(participants$bmi)
[1] 23
# The shorthand \(x) syntax - identical result
(\(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))(participants$bmi)
[1] 23
Both versions compute the exact same range width for bmi (38.6 − 15.6 = 23) without ever creating a named, reusable function object — appropriate here, since this calculation is only needed this one time.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.3 Explore
Try wrapping the same anonymous function around participants$age instead of participants$bmi. Notice you can reuse the pattern freely by copy-pasting it — what you’re avoiding isn’t reuse of the code itself, but the overhead of formally naming and storing a function object you’ll only call once.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.4 Solve the Problem
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

age_range_width <- (\(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))(participants$age)
bmi_range_width <- (\(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))(participants$bmi)

age_range_width
[1] 63
bmi_range_width
[1] 23
Both range widths (63 for age, 23 for BMI) computed correctly using the shorthand anonymous function syntax, with no named function ever created or stored for later reuse.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.5 Interpret the Result
Anonymous functions are most useful exactly in situations like this: a one-off calculation, or (as the next page shows) a quick calculation passed directly as an argument into another function like sapply(). Using a full named function definition for something this disposable would add unnecessary ceremony to genuinely one-time code.

Common Mistakes

Using an anonymous function for something you actually need to call repeatedly. If you find yourself copy-pasting the same anonymous function multiple times, that’s a signal (echoing Page 1) it should become a properly named function instead — anonymous functions are for genuine one-off use, not disguised repetition.
Forgetting the parentheses needed to call an anonymous function immediately. Writing \(x) x + 1(5) doesn’t call the function with 5 as intended — the whole anonymous function definition needs to be wrapped in its own parentheses first, then called: (\(x) x + 1)(5).
Confusing the modern \(x) shorthand with older R syntax. The backslash shorthand was introduced in R 4.1 — code written for older R versions will only use the full function(x) form; both are equivalent in current R, so either is fine to use going forward.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.6 Practice Problem
Write an anonymous function (using the \(x) shorthand) that computes the coefficient of variation (sd(x, na.rm = TRUE) / mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) * 100) and apply it immediately to participants$systolic_bp.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.7 Challenge Problem
Explain, in your own words, the difference between when you’d choose a named function (Pages 2-6) versus an anonymous one (this page). Give one example scenario from this course so far where each would be the more appropriate choice.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.8 Key Takeaways

An anonymous function has no name and can be defined and called immediately, appropriate for genuinely one-off calculations.
R’s \(x) shorthand is equivalent to the full function(x) syntax, just less typing — especially handy when passing a quick function directly as an argument to another function.
Repeated use of the same anonymous function is a signal it should become a properly named, reusable function instead.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does \(x) mean in modern R syntax?

A comment
Shorthand for defining an anonymous function
A file path separator
An error-checking operator

True or false. An anonymous function must always be assigned to a variable name before it can be called.
Predict the output. What would (\(x) x * 2)(10) return?
Short answer. What’s the clearest signal that an anonymous function should instead be rewritten as a named one?



Show answers


b) Shorthand for defining an anonymous function (equivalent to function(x)).
False. An anonymous function can be defined and called immediately, with no name ever assigned.
20.
If the same anonymous function is being copy-pasted and reused in multiple places, that repetition is the same signal from Page 1 indicating the code should become one properly named, reusable function instead.

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.1 The R Concept
sapply() (“simplify apply”) applies a function to every element of a vector or list — including every column of a data frame — and simplifies the result into a vector when possible:
lapply() (“list apply”) does the exact same thing, but always returns a list instead of simplifying — useful when the results don’t simplify neatly into a single vector:
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
sapply(df, function_name)

lapply(df, function_name)
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.2 Demonstration
pct_missing <- function(x) {
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sapply(participants, pct_missing)
         id         age         sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp
          0           6           0           0           4            0
        bmi      smoker
          5           0
One call, sapply(participants, pct_missing), applied pct_missing() to all 8 columns automatically — no more retyping pct_missing(participants$...) eight separate times.
lapply(participants, pct_missing)
$id
[1] 0

$age
[1] 6

$sex
[1] 0
... (output continues as a list, one entry per column)
lapply() produces the same underlying values, but as a list (one named entry per column) rather than sapply()’s simplified named vector.
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.3 Explore
Try sapply(participants, class) — applying the built-in class() function (Level 2) across every column instead of pct_missing(). This confirms sapply() works with any function, built-in or custom, as long as it’s designed to take a single column as input.
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.4 Solve the Problem
pct_missing <- function(x) {
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

missing_report <- sapply(participants, pct_missing)

missing_report
         id         age         sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp
          0           6           0           0           4            0
        bmi      smoker
          5           0
missing_report[missing_report > 0]
        age systolic_bp         bmi
          6           4           5
sapply() computed the missing-value percentage for all 8 columns in one call, and a follow-up logical filter (missing_report > 0, Level 2) immediately narrows the result down to just the 3 columns that actually have any missingness — age (6%), systolic_bp (4%), and bmi (5%).
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.5 Interpret the Result
This is exactly the level’s opening mission delivered: pct_missing() was written once (Page 2), and sapply() applied it across the entire dataset automatically — no repetition, no retyping the calculation eight times, and the result immediately usable for further filtering (missing_report > 0) to focus attention on only the columns that need it.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting sapply() and lapply() pass one column at a time to the function, not the whole data frame at once. pct_missing() receives participants$age as its x on one call, then participants$systolic_bp on the next — never the entire participants data frame in a single call.
Expecting sapply() to always return a clean vector. sapply() simplifies when it can, but if a function’s results don’t have a consistent length or type across every column, sapply() falls back to returning a list — the same result lapply() would have given directly.
Applying a function written for a single value (like bp_category(), Page 4) directly to an entire column without sapply(). bp_category()’s if statement expects one number, not a vector — calling bp_category(participants$systolic_bp) directly errors; wrapping it as sapply(participants$systolic_bp, bp_category) (or vectorizing the function itself) is what correctly applies it across every value.
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.6 Practice Problem
Use sapply(participants, class) to check the storage type of every column. Which columns are "character", and which are "integer" or "numeric"?
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.7 Challenge Problem
Write a function called n_unique that returns the number of distinct non-missing values in a vector (hint: use length(unique(...)) combined with removing NAs first). Apply it with sapply() across every column of participants, and confirm county shows exactly 5 distinct values, matching Level 4’s finding.
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: sapply() and lapply()
text:
118.8 Key Takeaways

sapply(df, function_name) applies a function to every column of a data frame, simplifying the result into a vector when possible.
lapply(df, function_name) does the same thing but always returns a list, useful when results don’t simplify neatly.
Both sapply() and lapply() pass one column at a time to the function — the function itself is still written exactly as if handling a single vector, exactly as on Pages 2-4.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does sapply(participants, pct_missing) do?

Applies pct_missing() to the entire data frame as one single input
Applies pct_missing() separately to each column, simplifying the results into a vector
Filters participants to only rows with missing values
Modifies participants in place

True or false. lapply() always returns a simplified vector, exactly like sapply().
Predict the output. Given sapply(participants, pct_missing)’s result, what would missing_report["bmi"] return?
Find the error.
bp_category(participants$systolic_bp)
# intended: classify every participant's blood pressure at once



Show answers


b) Applies pct_missing() separately to each column, simplifying the results into a vector.
False. lapply() always returns a list; sapply() attempts to simplify the result into a vector (or matrix) when possible.
5 — the percentage of missing bmi values.
bp_category() (Page 4) was written expecting a single value, using if, which errors on a vector input. It should be wrapped with sapply(): sapply(participants$systolic_bp, bp_category), applying the single-value function across every element correctly.
crumbs: 118  Applying a Function Across Columns: `sapply()` and `lapply()`

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.1 The R Concept
purrr::map() (part of the tidyverse) applies a function across a vector or data frame’s columns, always returning a list — predictable, but not simplified:
Its type-specific variants guarantee a specific output type, erroring loudly instead of silently returning the wrong shape if a function doesn’t produce what’s expected:
This predictability is purrr’s core advantage over base R’s sapply(): you know exactly what type of result to expect before you even run the code, and get an immediate, clear error if a function doesn’t honor that promise — rather than a silently different-shaped result to debug later.

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
library(purrr)
map(df, function_name)

map_dbl(df, function_name)   # always returns a numeric vector, or errors
map_chr(df, function_name)   # always returns a character vector, or errors

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.2 Demonstration
library(purrr)

pct_missing <- function(x) {
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

map_dbl(participants, pct_missing)
         id         age         sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp
          0           6           0           0           4            0
        bmi      smoker
          5           0
The exact same values sapply() produced on the previous page — but map_dbl() guarantees this is a numeric vector, since pct_missing() always returns a single number for any input.

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.3 Explore
Try map_chr(participants, class) — applying the built-in class() function (which always returns a character value) across every column. Since class() always produces text, map_chr() is the appropriately type-guaranteed choice here, rather than the more general map() or the numeric-specific map_dbl().

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.4 Solve the Problem
library(purrr)
library(dplyr)

pct_missing <- function(x) {
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

missing_report <- map_dbl(participants, pct_missing)

missing_report[missing_report > 0]
        age systolic_bp         bmi
          6           4           5
column_types <- map_chr(participants, class)

column_types
          id          age          sex       county  systolic_bp diastolic_bp
   "integer"    "integer"  "character"  "character"    "integer"    "integer"
         bmi       smoker
   "numeric"  "character"
Both the missing-value percentages and the column types are computed with guaranteed, predictable output types — map_dbl() promising numbers, map_chr() promising text — matching sapply()’s results from the previous page exactly, just with an explicit type contract attached.

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.5 Interpret the Result
The results are identical to sapply()’s (Page 8) — the practical difference only shows up when something unexpected happens. If pct_missing() accidentally returned text for one column (a bug), map_dbl() would immediately error, pointing you straight to the problem; sapply() might instead silently return a mismatched list, leaving you to notice the problem much later, and less obviously.

Common Mistakes

Choosing map() when a type-specific variant is available and appropriate. Plain map() always returns a list, even when every result is a single number — map_dbl() is almost always the better choice when you know every result should be numeric, since it also validates that promise.
Mismatching the variant to what the function actually returns. Using map_chr() on a function that returns numbers (like pct_missing()) causes an immediate error — the variant you choose must match what the applied function actually produces.
Forgetting library(purrr). Like every tidyverse package, purrr’s functions must be loaded first — though map(), map_dbl(), and map_chr() are also available automatically if library(tidyverse) was used instead of loading individual packages.

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.6 Practice Problem
Use map_dbl() to apply the range_width function (Page 2’s practice problem) across every numeric column of participants (hint: you may need to first select() (Level 5) only the numeric columns, since range_width() won’t work on character columns like county).

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.7 Challenge Problem
Compare sapply(participants, pct_missing) and map_dbl(participants, pct_missing) side by side using identical(). Are their results identical in this case? Then research (or predict) a scenario in which sapply()’s output would differ in type from map_dbl()’s, even if the underlying values were the same.

title: 119  The Tidyverse Way: purrr::map()
text:
119.8 Key Takeaways

purrr::map() and its type-specific variants (map_dbl(), map_chr()) apply a function across a data frame’s columns, similar to sapply()/lapply().
Type-specific variants guarantee a specific output type, erroring clearly if a function doesn’t honor that promise — more predictable than sapply()’s sometimes-simplifies, sometimes-doesn’t behavior.
Choosing the right variant (map_dbl() for numbers, map_chr() for text) should match what the applied function is actually known to return.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does map_dbl() guarantee about its return value?

Nothing — it behaves identically to sapply()
It always returns a numeric vector, or errors if the function doesn’t produce numbers
It always returns a list
It always returns a character vector

True or false. map() (with no suffix) always returns a list, regardless of what the applied function produces.
Predict the output. If pct_missing() always returns a single number, would map_dbl(participants, pct_missing) ever error?
Find the error.
map_chr(participants, pct_missing)
# pct_missing() returns numbers, not text



Show answers


b) It always returns a numeric vector, or errors if the applied function doesn’t produce numbers.
True. Plain map() always returns a list, unlike sapply(), which sometimes simplifies and sometimes doesn’t.
No — since pct_missing() reliably returns a single number for any valid input, map_dbl() would never error on it.
map_chr() expects the applied function to return character (text) values, but pct_missing() returns numbers — this mismatch causes map_chr() to error immediately. It should be map_dbl(participants, pct_missing) instead, matching the function’s actual (numeric) return type.

title: 120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
text:
120.1 The R Concept
stop() immediately halts a function’s execution and raises an error with a custom message — appropriate when the function genuinely cannot produce a meaningful result:
warning() prints a caution message but lets the function continue running — appropriate for situations worth flagging but not necessarily fatal:

title: 120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
text:
120.3 Explore
Try calling the updated pct_missing() on a valid column, like participants$age. Confirm it still works exactly as before for legitimate input — stop() only interrupts execution when the specific bad condition (length(x) == 0) is actually met, not for every call.

title: 120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
text:
120.5 Interpret the Result
pct_missing() now protects both the function’s caller and anyone reading its output: a stop()-triggered error message makes an invalid call immediately obvious rather than producing a confusing silent result, while a warning() flags a technically valid but concerning result (100% missing) without halting a larger script that might process many columns in a loop.

Common Mistakes

Using warning() when the situation actually warrants stop(). A warning() lets execution continue — if the function truly cannot produce a meaningful result (like the empty-vector case), continuing anyway just produces a bad result further downstream; stop() is the right choice whenever continuing would be actively misleading.
Writing a vague error message. stop("Error!") tells a caller nothing about what actually went wrong or how to fix it — a good stop() message states the specific problem, as in "x has length 0 - cannot compute percent missing".
Adding so much validation that a function becomes hard to use for legitimate edge cases. Not every unusual input needs a stop() or warning() — reserve them for situations that are genuinely invalid or genuinely worth flagging, not just unusual.

title: 120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
text:
120.6 Practice Problem
Add a stop() check to bp_category() (Page 4) that halts execution with a clear message if sbp is negative (an impossible blood pressure reading). Test it with a normal value and a negative one.

title: 120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
text:
120.7 Challenge Problem
Modify is_at_risk() (Page 5) to warning() (not stop()) if age_cutoff is set below 0 or above 120 (an implausible age threshold), while still allowing the function to run. Explain why warning() is more appropriate than stop() for this particular situation, compared to the empty-vector case above.

title: 120  Basic Error Handling: stop() and warning()
text:
120.8 Key Takeaways

stop() immediately halts a function with a custom error message, appropriate when the function truly cannot produce a meaningful result.
warning() prints a caution message but allows the function to continue running, appropriate for concerning-but-not-fatal situations.
A good error or warning message states the specific problem clearly, helping whoever encounters it fix the actual issue quickly.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What is the key difference between stop() and warning()?

stop() prints a message and continues; warning() halts execution
stop() halts execution; warning() prints a message and continues
They behave identically
warning() can only be used inside if statements

True or false. A function should use stop() for every unusual or unexpected input, no matter how minor.
Predict the output. What would pct_missing(numeric(0)) do, given the version of the function with the length(x) == 0 check added?
Short answer. Why is a specific, descriptive error message (like "x has length 0 - cannot compute percent missing") more useful than a generic one (like "Error!")?



Show answers


b) stop() halts execution with an error; warning() prints a message but lets the function continue running.
False. Overusing stop()/warning() for minor or merely unusual inputs makes a function harder to use — reserve them for genuinely invalid or genuinely concerning situations.
It immediately stops with the error message "x has length 0 - cannot compute percent missing", rather than returning a confusing NaN.
A specific message tells whoever encounters the error exactly what went wrong and often hints at how to fix it, saving significant debugging time compared to a generic message that provides no clue about the actual problem.

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
text:
121.1 The R Concept
A complete, well-built function combines every idea from this level: input validation (Page 10) first, then conditional logic (Page 4) branching on the input’s type, using default arguments (Page 3) for adjustable behavior, all wrapped as one named, reusable tool (Page 2) that can be applied across many columns at once (Page 8-9):
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
text:
121.3 Explore
Try column_report(participants$smoker) and column_report(participants$bmi). Confirm the numeric branch and character branch each produce sensible, correctly formatted output — and try column_report(numeric(0)) to confirm the stop() check still works exactly as it did on Page 10.
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
text:
121.4 Solve the Problem
library(purrr)

column_report <- function(x, digits = 2) {
  if (length(x) == 0) {
    stop("x has length 0 - cannot build a report")
  }
  pct_miss <- round(sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100, digits)

  if (is.numeric(x)) {
    paste0("numeric | ", pct_miss, "% missing | mean = ",
           round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits), ", sd = ",
           round(sd(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits))
  } else {
    paste0("character | ", pct_miss, "% missing | ",
           length(unique(x[!is.na(x)])), " unique values")
  }
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

full_report <- map_chr(participants, column_report)

full_report
                    id                    age                    sex
"numeric | 0% missing... "numeric | 6% missing... "character | 0% mi...
                county            systolic_bp           diastolic_bp
"character | 0% mi...  "numeric | 4% missing...  "numeric | 0% missing...
                   bmi                 smoker
"numeric | 5% missing... "character | 0% mi...
full_report["age"]
                                                  age
"numeric | 6% missing | mean = 47.43, sd = 15.03"
full_report["county"]
                                          county
"character | 0% missing | 5 unique values"
One map_chr() call generated a complete, correctly branched report for all 8 columns at once — the exact tool your professor asked for, built entirely from pieces developed one at a time across this level.
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
text:
121.5 Interpret the Result
column_report() correctly identifies age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, and bmi as numeric (reporting mean and SD), and sex, county, and smoker as character (reporting the count of distinct categories) — with id, though numeric, also handled correctly by the same branch, even though a mean and SD of an ID number isn’t particularly meaningful. This is worth noting as a real limitation: the function correctly determines type, but doesn’t know which numeric columns are meaningfully continuous (like age) versus arbitrary identifiers (like id) — a distinction still requiring your own judgment as the analyst.

Common Mistakes

Assuming a function that runs without errors is automatically producing meaningful output. column_report(participants$id) runs successfully and reports a mean and SD, but an ID column’s “average” isn’t a meaningful statistic — a function correctly executing its logic isn’t the same as that logic being the right thing to compute for every column.
Forgetting is.numeric() and is.character() aren’t the only possible column types. This function’s if (is.numeric(x)) {...} else {...} treats everything non-numeric as the character branch — a logical (TRUE/FALSE) or factor column would fall into that same else branch, potentially producing a less appropriate summary than a dedicated branch for that type would.
Building one big function instead of composing several small, tested ones. column_report() reuses ideas (validation, conditionals, rounding) developed and tested separately across this level’s earlier pages — building complex functionality out of smaller, already-verified pieces is more reliable than writing one large function from scratch in a single attempt.
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
text:
121.6 Practice Problem
Modify column_report() to accept a digits value of 1 instead of the default 2, and confirm the numeric branch’s mean/SD values change precision accordingly for participants$systolic_bp.
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
text:
121.7 Challenge Problem
Extend column_report() with a third branch specifically for logical (TRUE/FALSE) columns, reporting the percentage TRUE instead of a mean/SD or unique-value count. Since participants doesn’t currently have a logical column, first create one with mutate(is_senior = age >= 65) (Level 5) to test your new branch.
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report
text:
121.8 Key Takeaways

A well-built function combines input validation, conditional branching, sensible defaults, and clear return values — each individually simple, together forming a genuinely useful tool.
A function running without error doesn’t guarantee its output is meaningful for every possible input — type-checking logic (is.numeric()) doesn’t know which numeric columns are conceptually continuous versus arbitrary identifiers.
Building complex functionality by composing smaller, already-tested pieces (as this capstone did, reusing ideas from Pages 2-10) is more reliable than writing one large function from scratch.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does column_report()’s if (is.numeric(x)) branch handle?

Only columns explicitly named “numeric”
Any column where is.numeric() returns TRUE, like age or bmi
Character columns only
Columns with no missing values

True or false. A function running without producing an error guarantees its output is meaningful for every input.
Predict the output. What would column_report(participants$sex) report, given sex has 2 distinct values and no missing data?
Short answer. Why is id’s mean and SD, while technically computed correctly, not a particularly meaningful piece of information?



Show answers


b) Any column where is.numeric() returns TRUE — this includes id, age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, and bmi.
False. A function can execute successfully and still produce output that isn’t meaningful for a particular input, as column_report(participants$id) demonstrates.
"character | 0% missing | 2 unique values".
id is an arbitrary identifier, not a meaningfully continuous measurement — its “average” value (50.5) doesn’t describe anything about the participants themselves, unlike age’s mean, which genuinely summarizes a real characteristic of the sample.
crumbs: 121  Level 9 Capstone: A Complete Data Quality Report

title: Level 10 — Loops and Automation
text:
What you’ll build toward
Level 9 already covered applying a function you write across many columns at once (sapply(), purrr::map()). This level covers the more general tool underneath: the explicit loop — for and while — plus two base R loop-replacements Level 9 didn’t reach (vapply(), tapply()), and, crucially, when a loop is actually the right tool versus when a vectorized approach (Level 2 onward) is faster and more readable. You’ll finish by automating a genuinely repetitive real task: your data arrives as five separate, identically structured clinic files, and you’ll write the loop that processes all five in one run.

title: Level 10 — Loops and Automation
text:
Topics in this level

Why explicit loops matter, even after learning sapply()/map() in Level 9
for loops: iterating over a vector or sequence
Accumulating results across loop iterations
while loops: repeating until a condition is no longer true
Looping over a data frame’s columns
Nested loops (a loop inside a loop)
vapply() (a stricter sapply()) and tapply() (grouped summaries without group_by())
When to loop, and when to prefer a vectorized approach instead
A capstone: automating the same cleaning steps across five separate files

title: Level 10 — Loops and Automation
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: your team collects data from five clinics every month, each landing in an identically structured file (data/clinics/clinic_*.csv). Automate reading, checking, and combining all five into one dataset with a single loop, so the process takes one run instead of five separate ones — and confirm the combined result exactly reproduces the familiar 100-participant participants.csv you’ve used since Level 1.

title: 122  for Loops
text:
122.1 The R Concept
A for loop repeats a block of code once for every element in a vector, giving each element a temporary name you choose:
Each time through the loop, item takes on the next value in vector, in order, until every element has been used once.

title: 122  for Loops
122  for Loops
text:
122.2 Demonstration
counties <- c("Cedar Falls", "Millbrook", "Oakford", "Riverdale", "Springvale")

for (county in counties) {
  print(county)
}
[1] "Cedar Falls"
[1] "Millbrook"
[1] "Oakford"
[1] "Riverdale"
[1] "Springvale"
The loop ran exactly 5 times — once per county name — printing each one in the same order it appeared in counties, with county taking on each successive value automatically.

title: 122  for Loops
text:
122.3 Explore
Try looping over 1:5 instead of the county names: for (i in 1:5) { print(i * 10) }. Confirm the loop runs exactly 5 times, with i taking values 1 through 5 in order — the loop variable doesn’t have to be a meaningful real-world value; it’s often just a counter.

title: 122  for Loops
text:
122.4 Solve the Problem
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

distinct_counties <- unique(participants$county)

for (county in distinct_counties) {
  n_participants <- sum(participants$county == county)
  print(paste(county, ":", n_participants, "participants"))
}
[1] "Oakford : 17 participants"
[1] "Riverdale : 16 participants"
[1] "Millbrook : 21 participants"
[1] "Springvale : 26 participants"
[1] "Cedar Falls : 20 participants"
The loop ran once per distinct county (in the order unique() first encountered them in the data, not alphabetically), computing and printing each county’s participant count — matching exactly the counts Level 4’s table() and Level 5’s count() already established.

title: 122  for Loops
text:
122.5 Interpret the Result
This for loop reproduces information you already had from table(participants$county) (Level 4) — but explicitly, one county at a time, with full control over exactly what happens on each iteration (here, a custom formatted sentence rather than a bare count). This explicitness is both the loop’s strength (you can do anything inside it) and its verbosity (Level 9’s sapply()/map() would get you the raw counts in one line, without a loop at all).

Common Mistakes

Forgetting a for loop’s results disappear unless explicitly saved. print() inside the loop displays each value as it runs, but doesn’t save anything — if you need the results after the loop finishes, you must store them somewhere (Page 2 covers exactly this).
Looping over the wrong thing. for (county in nrow(participants)) loops over a single number (the row count), not over each county — a common typo-like mistake is looping over a summary of the data instead of the data (or its distinct values) itself.
Assuming a for loop is always the best tool. As Level 9 showed, sapply()/map() often replace a for loop entirely, more concisely — for loops remain most useful when you need explicit step-by-step control, or when later steps depend on earlier ones (Page 7 explores this distinction further).

title: 122  for Loops
text:
122.6 Practice Problem
Write a for loop over unique(participants$smoker) that prints each smoking status alongside its count, similar to the county example above.

title: 122  for Loops
text:
122.7 Challenge Problem
Write a for loop over 1:5 that prints the squares of 1 through 5 (i^2). Then rewrite the same task using sapply(1:5, function(i) i^2) (Level 9) and confirm both approaches produce the same 5 values, just via different mechanics.

title: 122  for Loops
text:
122.8 Key Takeaways

for (item in vector) { ... } repeats a block of code once per element of vector, with item taking each successive value in turn.
A for loop’s results are not automatically saved anywhere — anything you want to keep must be explicitly stored (next page).
for loops offer full explicit control at the cost of verbosity — Level 9’s sapply()/map() often replace simple loops more concisely, but loops remain useful for more complex, step-dependent tasks.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In for (county in counties) { print(county) }, how many times does the loop run?

Exactly once
Once per element of counties
Infinitely, until manually stopped
Zero times, since nothing is returned

True or false. Values printed inside a for loop are automatically saved to a vector for later use.
Predict the output. How many times would for (i in 1:5) { print(i) } print a value?
Find the error.
for (county in nrow(participants)) {
  print(county)
}
# intended: print each distinct county name



Show answers


b) Once per element of counties — 5 times, for 5 county names.
False. print() only displays values as the loop runs; nothing is saved unless you explicitly store it (Page 2).
5 times, printing 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in order.
nrow(participants) is a single number (100), so the loop runs only once, printing 100 — not once per distinct county. It should loop over unique(participants$county) instead.

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.1 The R Concept
To accumulate results, create an empty (or appropriately sized) vector before the loop starts, then assign into a specific position of it during each iteration:
seq_along(x) generates the sequence 1, 2, 3, ..., length(x) — useful for looping by position (needed here, since you must know which slot in results to fill on each iteration) rather than by value directly.
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.2 Demonstration
counties <- unique(participants$county)

county_counts <- vector("numeric", length(counties))   # pre-allocate 5 empty slots
names(county_counts) <- counties                        # label each slot

for (i in seq_along(counties)) {
  county_counts[i] <- sum(participants$county == counties[i])
}

county_counts
    Oakford   Riverdale   Millbrook  Springvale Cedar Falls
         17          16          21          26          20
Unlike the previous page’s print()-only loop, county_counts now genuinely exists as a named vector after the loop finishes — usable for further calculations, exactly like any other vector from Level 2.
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.3 Explore
Try sum(county_counts) after running the loop above. It should equal 100, confirming the accumulated counts across all five counties correctly sum to the full sample size — a useful sanity check whenever accumulating results across groups that should account for the whole dataset.
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.4 Solve the Problem
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

numeric_columns <- c("age", "systolic_bp", "diastolic_bp", "bmi")

pct_missing_results <- vector("numeric", length(numeric_columns))
names(pct_missing_results) <- numeric_columns

for (i in seq_along(numeric_columns)) {
  col_name <- numeric_columns[i]
  col_values <- participants[[col_name]]
  pct_missing_results[i] <- sum(is.na(col_values)) / length(col_values) * 100
}

pct_missing_results
        age systolic_bp diastolic_bp         bmi
          6           4            0           5
The exact same percentages Level 9’s sapply(participants, pct_missing) produced — but built here explicitly, one column at a time, with the result accumulated into a pre-allocated vector rather than handled automatically.
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.5 Interpret the Result
pct_missing_results now exists as a genuine, reusable vector, matching the missing-value percentages already established in Level 9 (6% age, 4% systolic BP, 0% diastolic BP, 5% BMI) — confirming the explicit loop-and-accumulate approach and Level 9’s sapply() shortcut agree exactly, as they should, since they’re computing the identical thing by different means.

Common Mistakes

Growing a vector inside the loop instead of pre-allocating it. Writing results <- c(results, new_value) inside a loop (rather than pre-allocating results at the correct size beforehand) works, but is slower and considered poor style for larger loops — pre-allocating with vector("numeric", n) first is the more robust habit to build.
Looping over values instead of positions when position matters. for (col_name in numeric_columns) (looping by value) makes it harder to know which slot of a pre-allocated results vector to fill — for (i in seq_along(numeric_columns)) (looping by position) solves this directly, since i gives you both the current position and (via indexing) the current value.
Forgetting to name the results vector. pct_missing_results without names() assigned would just be an unlabeled vector of four numbers — much harder to interpret than one clearly labeled by column name.
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.6 Practice Problem
Write a loop that accumulates the mean value of each numeric column (age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi) into a named results vector, using seq_along() and pre-allocation as shown above.
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.7 Challenge Problem
Modify the county-count loop above to also accumulate each county’s mean systolic_bp into a second pre-allocated vector, alongside the participant counts. Confirm your accumulated means match Level 5’s group_by(county) + summarise() results (Cedar Falls 128.16, Millbrook 133.30, Oakford 129.71, Riverdale 124.36, Springvale 131.46).
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop
text:
123.8 Key Takeaways

Accumulating results requires creating a container (typically pre-allocated with vector()) before the loop starts, then assigning into a specific position during each iteration.
seq_along(x) generates position indices (1, 2, ..., length(x)), useful whenever you need to know which slot to fill, not just the current value.
Pre-allocating a results vector at the correct size is more efficient and more robust than growing a vector one element at a time inside a loop.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does seq_along(c("a", "b", "c")) return?

c("a", "b", "c")
c(1, 2, 3)
3
c(0, 1, 2)

True or false. Values printed with print() inside a loop are automatically available in a vector after the loop finishes.
Predict the output. If results <- vector("numeric", 5) before a loop runs, what are the initial values of results, before any assignment happens?
Find the error.
pct_missing_results <- vector("numeric", length(numeric_columns))
for (col_name in numeric_columns) {
  pct_missing_results[col_name] <- sum(is.na(participants[[col_name]])) / nrow(participants) * 100
}



Show answers


b) c(1, 2, 3) — the position indices, not the values themselves.
False. print() only displays values as the loop runs — nothing is saved to a vector unless explicitly assigned into one.
All zeros: c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0) — vector("numeric", 5) pre-allocates 5 slots, each initialized to 0.
This isn’t strictly wrong — R allows indexing a vector by name (pct_missing_results[col_name]), which would actually work correctly here despite looping by value instead of position — but it’s a more fragile pattern than looping with seq_along(), since results[col_name] silently creates a new named element if col_name doesn’t already exist as one of the vector’s names, rather than erroring on a genuine mismatch.
crumbs: 123  Accumulating Results in a Loop

title: 124  while Loops
text:
124.1 The R Concept
A while loop repeats a block of code for as long as a condition remains TRUE, checking that condition again before every iteration:
Unlike a for loop, a while loop doesn’t know in advance how many times it will run — it simply keeps going until its condition becomes FALSE. This makes while loops appropriate for exactly the kind of “keep going until…” question for loops aren’t naturally suited for.

title: 124  while Loops
124  while Loops
text:
124.2 Demonstration
count <- 0

while (count < 5) {
  print(count)
  count <- count + 1
}
[1] 0
[1] 1
[1] 2
[1] 3
[1] 4
The loop checks count < 5 before every iteration, stopping the moment count reaches 5 — note that count <- count + 1 inside the loop is what eventually makes the condition FALSE; without it, this loop would run forever (an “infinite loop,” a common bug to watch for).

title: 124  while Loops
text:
124.3 Explore
Try removing the count <- count + 1 line from the loop above (don’t actually run it, just consider it). Since count would never change, count < 5 would remain TRUE forever — this is exactly why every while loop needs something inside it that moves its condition toward becoming FALSE.

title: 124  while Loops
text:
124.4 Solve the Problem
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ages <- sort(participants$age[!is.na(participants$age)], decreasing = TRUE)

removed_count <- 0

while (mean(ages) >= 40) {
  ages <- ages[-1]           # remove the current oldest (first, since sorted descending)
  removed_count <- removed_count + 1
}

removed_count
[1] 29
mean(ages)
[1] 39.95385
length(ages)
[1] 65
The loop removed the oldest participant, one at a time, checking the mean age after each removal — stopping after exactly 29 removals, once the remaining 65 participants’ mean age dropped to 39.95, just below the 40 threshold.

title: 124  while Loops
text:
124.5 Interpret the Result
Nearly a third of the original 94 non-missing ages (29 of 94) had to be removed — specifically, the oldest third — before the sample’s mean age dropped below 40. This is a genuinely different kind of question than anything answerable with a for loop directly: you needed to keep going until a condition was met, with no way to know in advance exactly how many iterations that would take.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting to include something that changes the condition inside the loop. This is the single most common while loop bug — an infinite loop, where the condition never becomes FALSE because nothing inside the loop actually moves toward that outcome.
Using while when a for loop (or vectorized approach) would be simpler. If you already know exactly how many iterations you need, a for loop is usually clearer — while loops are best reserved for situations where the stopping point genuinely depends on a condition evaluated during the loop, not a known count in advance.
Not having an escape plan for a while loop that might never terminate. If there’s any chance the condition could never become FALSE (a data problem, a logic error), consider adding a safety counter (e.g., stop after 1000 iterations regardless) to prevent a truly infinite loop from freezing your session.

title: 124  while Loops
text:
124.6 Practice Problem
Write a while loop that removes the youngest participant, one at a time, until the remaining sample’s mean age exceeds 55. How many participants need to be removed?

title: 124  while Loops
text:
124.7 Challenge Problem
Modify the blood-pressure equivalent of this question: starting from all non-missing systolic_bp values, repeatedly remove the single highest reading until the mean drops below 125. How many removals are required, and what does this suggest about how much a handful of high readings are pulling up the overall mean?

title: 124  while Loops
text:
124.8 Key Takeaways

while (condition) { ... } repeats a block of code for as long as condition remains TRUE, without needing to know the iteration count in advance.
Every while loop must contain something that eventually makes its condition FALSE — omitting this creates an infinite loop.
while loops are the right tool specifically when a stopping point depends on a condition evaluated during the loop, unlike for loops (Page 1), which need a known number of iterations up front.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. When is a while loop the more appropriate choice over a for loop?

When you know exactly how many iterations you need in advance
When the number of iterations depends on a condition that’s only known while the loop runs
Never — for loops can always replace while loops
Only when looping over character vectors

True or false. Every while loop is guaranteed to eventually stop on its own, without any special care needed.
Predict the output. If count <- 0 and the loop is while (count < 3) { print(count); count <- count + 1 }, how many times does it print?
Find the error.
count <- 0
while (count < 5) {
  print(count)
}



Show answers


b) When the number of iterations depends on a condition that’s only known while the loop runs, not a fixed count decided in advance.
False. A while loop only stops if something inside it actually changes the condition toward FALSE — without that, it runs forever (an infinite loop).
3 times, printing 0, 1, 2.
count is never updated inside the loop, so count < 5 remains TRUE forever, creating an infinite loop that would need to be manually interrupted. It should include count <- count + 1 inside the loop body.

title: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame’s Columns
text:
125.1 The R Concept
Looping over a data frame’s columns means looping over its column names, then using double-bracket indexing (df[[col_name]]) to access each column’s actual values — df$col_name doesn’t work here, because $ requires the literal column name typed directly, not a variable holding that name as text:
crumbs: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame's Columns

title: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame’s Columns
crumbs: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame's Columns

title: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame’s Columns
text:
125.2 Demonstration
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

for (col_name in names(participants)) {
  print(col_name)
}
[1] "id"
[1] "age"
[1] "sex"
[1] "county"
[1] "systolic_bp"
[1] "diastolic_bp"
[1] "bmi"
[1] "smoker"
The loop visits all 8 column names in order — but printing just the names isn’t very useful yet; the next step is using each name to access that column’s actual values.
crumbs: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame's Columns

title: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame’s Columns
text:
125.3 Explore
Try participants$col_name inside the loop (instead of participants[[col_name]]) and see what happens. Since col_name is a variable holding text like "age", $ looks for a column literally named col_name, which doesn’t exist — df[[col_name]] is what correctly uses the value of col_name to select a column.
crumbs: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame's Columns

title: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame’s Columns
text:
125.4 Solve the Problem
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

for (col_name in names(participants)) {
  column_values <- participants[[col_name]]
  pct_miss <- round(sum(is.na(column_values)) / length(column_values) * 100, 1)
  print(paste0(col_name, ": ", pct_miss, "% missing"))
}
[1] "id: 0% missing"
[1] "age: 6% missing"
[1] "sex: 0% missing"
[1] "county: 0% missing"
[1] "systolic_bp: 4% missing"
[1] "diastolic_bp: 0% missing"
[1] "bmi: 5% missing"
[1] "smoker: 0% missing"
Exactly the same missing-value percentages Level 9’s sapply(participants, pct_missing) produced — now built with an explicit for loop over column names, using [[ ]] to access each column’s actual values by name.
crumbs: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame's Columns

title: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame’s Columns
text:
125.5 Interpret the Result
This confirms what sapply() was doing internally all along: visiting each column by name, applying a calculation, and (in sapply()’s case) automatically collecting the results into a vector. Writing the loop out explicitly makes every step visible — useful for understanding, debugging, or handling situations more complex than a single function call per column can express cleanly.

Common Mistakes

Using $ with a variable holding a column name. participants$col_name looks for a literal column called col_name, not the column whose name is stored in the col_name variable — this is one of the most common loop-over-columns mistakes, and [[ ]] is the fix.
Forgetting names() returns column names as text, not the columns themselves. for (col_name in names(participants)) gives you col_name as a string like "age" — you still need participants[[col_name]] to get the actual values.
Writing a loop when sapply()/map_dbl() (Level 9) would be simpler for a single, uniform calculation. This page’s loop is intentionally the “long way” to build understanding — for straightforward one-function-per-column tasks, Level 9’s shortcuts remain the more practical everyday choice.
crumbs: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame's Columns

title: 125  Looping Over a Data Frame’s Columns
text:
125.6 Practice Problem
